issue_title: Deserializing data in packet as a JSON should be optional.
issue_body:
In [packet.py](https://github.com/miguelgrinberg/python-engineio/blob/268e3cba424391dd32950d0ba08d28a65c4ef14b/engineio/packet.py#L83-L87), parser always try to deserialize data as JSON, even though engine.io protocol does not specify about JSON data.

I'm actually using python-engineio's packet class, and python-socketio's one to make socket.io client for python.
When I try to parse a message like `40` which means _connected_ packet `[type=CONNECT(0)]` in socket.io level, python-socketio's parser fails because of following steps.

1. python-engineio's parser parses `40` as a packet `[type=MESSAGE(4), data=0(integer)]` cause it parsed `0` as JSON string `0`
2. It put the data of engineio packet which is `0` to python-socketio's packet like `socketio.packet.Packet(encoded_packet=data)`
3. The non-zero test fails at [here](https://github.com/miguelgrinberg/python-socketio/blob/7f02f7aaa92c18c043466cb4721356221361f481/socketio/packet.py#L42) and then it parsed as a packet `[type=EVENT(2), data=None]`

It seems that it doesn't matter in server-side implementation. But, as I think, the parser should work for all valid cases.

issue_title: REST framework 3.4 - Schema generation.
issue_body:
Opening this up in order to start the conversation...

So, I'm working towards a 3.4 release. The headline features here are schema generation and client libraries.

Pull request: https://github.com/tomchristie/django-rest-framework/pull/4179
Upcoming new tutorial section: https://github.com/tomchristie/django-rest-framework/blob/schema-support/docs/tutorial/7-schemas-and-client-libraries.md

I think the client library support in particular is a big deal, and using Core API as the internal representation format is a necessary part of that.

Essentially we'll have a single built-in method for handling introspection, that returns a Core API `Document` object representing the schema. Various different renderers can then be built in order to translate the Core API `Document` into either a rendered set of documentation, or into a rendered schema, such as a swagger document.

* `django-rest-swagger` *could* move away from providing it's own introspection, and rely on what's in core, instead just handling the rendering of a Core API document, and URLs/views for serving the documentation templates that swagger provides.
* There will very likely be a `coreapi-openapi` package, that provides a `Document`<->`Swagger/OpenAPI` codec. (right now the `coreapi` package has swagger decoding built-in, but I'd really like to pull out the individual schema formats, with the exception of corejson, which serves as a canonical representation format), so `django-rest-swagger` could additionally rely on that for the rendering, providing just the wrapping for a REST framework renderer that uses the codec, plus the URLs/views for serving the documentation templates that swagger provides.

There's also some provisos that this introduces...

Core API can be mapped pretty well onto a swagger document, but there will be a few places where we might need to smooth things over a bit. Eg. we don't represent the expected status codes of a response in a link, because it's not actually behaviorally necessary in order to drive a dynamic client. We can consider how to best handle these aspects as we come to them.

Anyways, I'll leave it at that for now. Any thoughts/feedback welcome. The `django-rest-swagger` could just stay exactly as it is of course and all would be fine, but this is an interesting oppurtunity for us to make it much easier for developers to support a wide range of schema formats and documentation styles, and it might be worth considering if we could transition django-rest-swagger to leveraging the built-in schema support.

issue_title: Multiple copies of django-logo.svg created
issue_body:
When using this library with `django-storages` and [`AWS_S3_FILE_OVERWRITE=True`](http://django-storages.readthedocs.io/en/latest/backends/amazon-S3.html?highlight=AWS_S3_FILE_OVERWRITE), the library creates (a lot of) copies of the image `django-logo.svg`. We had 20,000+ created in a few weeks time.

This `django-storages` setting is to make sure that an upload of a file will not override existing file. It's useful as many people might upload a file called "photo.jpg" for different purposes.

I suspect is due to the `Theme.get_active_theme()` which is used in many places:
- post_migrate signal
- post_save signal
- post_delete signal
- template tag

Also, `get_active_theme`, might call `set_active`, which calls save, which calls `get_active_theme`. Looks like there might be a case where it's called several times in a loop. From an API standpoint, it's a lot of things for a method which apparently is just "getting" something.

Maybe it would make more sense to not do too many things in that `get_active_theme` method, separate the auto-updates in a setter method and provide sensible defaults in the templates if no theme is provided (using `{{ color|default:"#0C4B33" }}`)?

Sorry for the long issue, happy to discuss further as well as helping with the implementation.

issue_title: Wrong AJAX url for admin sorting
issue_body:
Currently for every registered model there is attached url with model_id parameter. All of these URLs will be with the same name and when resolved in template will point to last registered. For example if I have `app1` with models `App1Model1` and `App1Model2` and `app2` with models `App2Model1` and `App2Model2` and all of them are `Sortable` and registered in admin I will have these URLs:

    /admin/app1/model1/sorting/do-sorting/<model_id>/
    /admin/app1/model2/sorting/do-sorting/<model_id>/
    /admin/app2/model1/sorting/do-sorting/<model_id>/
    /admin/app2/model2/sorting/do-sorting/<model_id>/

Now I can order all models on all urls. For example if `App1Model2` is with `model_id==2` then  I can order the items in any of this URLs:

    /admin/app1/model1/sorting/do-sorting/2/
    /admin/app1/model2/sorting/do-sorting/2/
    /admin/app2/model1/sorting/do-sorting/2/
    /admin/app2/model2/sorting/do-sorting/2/

The same goes for any other model. In fact all models will be sorted in the URL registered last. For example if I register `App2Model2` last then all models will be sorted at the urls under:

    /admin/app2/model2/sorting/do-sorting/<model_id>

This all goes with AJAX and is unnoticed for users but is somehow counter intuitive.
Also there is no permission check which which will restrict access for sorting to people which does not have change permissions to given model.

The correct way of doing this will be to do the sorting for every model in the respective URLs. For example:

    /admin/app1/model1/sorting/do-sorting/
    /admin/app1/model2/sorting/do-sorting/
    /admin/app2/model1/sorting/do-sorting/
    /admin/app2/model2/sorting/do-sorting/

I will try to prepare PR for that.

One question though - Which is the minimum Django version that is supported?

issue_title: Support IAM integration
issue_body: Cloudant client libraries need to be able to exchange an IAM API key for an IAM access token. The user of the client library provides the API key. The access token is obtained by calling an IAM endpoint. The access token is time limited, like a cookie, so refresh logic also needs writing.

issue_title: Fix CloudantDatabase.share_database to include all roles
issue_body:
This issue splits out part of issue #52.  This issue deals with the fact that the `share_database` method of the `CloudantDatabase` class only supports the _reader, _writer, and _admin roles when sharing a database.  It needs to support all of the supported [roles](https://docs.cloudant.com/authorization.html#roles).

- The method signature should be changed to `share_database(self, username, perms=['_reader'])` 
- The `perms` argument will be a list of roles to apply to the `username` provided.
- If `perms` is ommitted then the default role of `_reader` will be applied.

issue_body:

When using Gluon APIs gluon.nn.SymbolBlock to load a pretrained VGG16 and finetune the model (while replacing the last VGG16 layer with an additional FullyConnected layer) the model file (.json) can be correctly saved, but not the parameters (weights, biases) with net.save_params().
The resulting file is of size 32Byte (no matter what size the additional layer) containing the only binary data:

Model used:

## Environment info (Required)
Ubuntu 16.04, MXNet version 1.2.0 (pip3 compiled version)

## Minimum reproducible example
    context = mx.cpu() # same for mx.gpu(0)
    data = mx.sym.var('data')
    sym, arg_params, aux_params = mx.model.load_checkpoint(<model-path>, 0)
    layers = sym.get_internals()
    sym = layers['fc8' + '_output']

issue_title: Overhead of MXNDArraySyncCopyFromCPU on osx
issue_body:
While investigating a performance issue I noticed that setting the values
of a `mx.nd.NDArray` is somewhat slow os osx (sierra):

```python
import mxnet as mx
import numpy as np
import ctypes

a = mx.nd.zeros(4)
b = np.zeros(4, dtype='f')
%timeit a[:] = b
28.3 µs ± 653 ns per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 10000 loops each)
```
For comparison, pure numpy takes about 400ns.
Some of this seems to be python overhead (the largest ones I found were `a.shape` with about 2μs and `a.ctypes.data_as(ctypes.c_void_p)` with 4μs in `a._sync_copyfrom`. Most of it is on the C side however:
```python
handle = a.handle
b_addr = b.ctypes.data_as(ctypes.c_void_p)
b_size = ctypes.c_size_t(b.size)
%timeit mx.base._LIB.MXNDArraySyncCopyFromCPU(handle, b_addr, b_size)
14.3 µs ± 1.66 µs per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 10000 loops each)
```
On a linux machine that same test runs in 900ns.

I am using version `0.11.1` according to `mx.__version__`, installed via `pip install mxnet-mkl`.

I sampled the stack trace while `MXNDArraySyncCopyFromCPU` was running in a loop:
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1882397/31045828-67a8aa42-a5ec-11e7-8465-c5711749f55c.png)

issue_title: NPE in BucketingModule when providedLabel of DataBatch is not set
issue_body:
We create a DataBatch without setting the `providedLabel` field. We then call `BucketingModule.forward(...)` with the data batch and get a NullPointerException:
```
java.lang.NullPointerException was thrown.
java.lang.NullPointerException
	at ml.dmlc.mxnet.DataDesc$.ListMap2Descs(IO.scala:252)
	at ml.dmlc.mxnet.module.BucketingModule.forward(BucketingModule.scala:313)
```

This happens because the implicit method `ListMap2Descs` tries to convert `ListMap[String, Shape]` into `IndexedSeq[DataDesc]` but fails because the former is null by default.

issue_title: conventions for symbol attributes
issue_body:
Since attributes are used more and more frequently by different components, do you guys think it would be better to have some naming conventions? @piiswrong @tqchen @mli @antinucleon , here are some conventions that have never been documented anywhere:
- Internal variables with double underscopes (e.g. `__shape__` is internally used to store optional shape information for a variable).
- Namespace: currently `list_attrs` use `_` as a separator between the symbol name and actually attribute name. I think since `_` might be very commonly be used in the attribute names themselves, we might want to choose a more distinct name, such as `:` or `.` or `/`.

Are there any other cases we need to consider?

Also when composing symbols. Currently the attributes are set **before** calling `_compose`. As a results, some attributes are implicitly created for children nodes. For example:

``` python
    data = mx.sym.Variable('data', attr={'mood': 'angry'})
    op = mx.sym.Convolution(data=data, name='conv', kernel=(1, 1),
                            num_filter=1, attr={'mood': 'so so'})
    assert op.list_attr() == {'data_mood': 'angry', 'conv_mood': 'so so',
                              'conv_weight_mood': 'so so', 'conv_bias_mood': 'so so'}
```

Note those `conv_weight_mood`, etc. I could imaging this might be useful in the case of `ctx2grp`. But @tqchen can you confirm this is expected behavior? Because we recently got a PR from the Julia side that changed the order of `_compose` and `setattr`, and I am wondering if I should switch it back.

issue_title: [OP] Input gamma mutated in Batch Norm
issue_body:
In BatchNorm operator, gamma is not listed as `aux_data` in backend, but actually mutated when `fix_gamma` is True. For sparse storage fallback, when a dense executors see a mutable sparse input, it will generate a dense copy, execute fcompute, and convert the mutated dense copy back to source NDArray. For BatchNorm gamma is not listed as mutable inputs, so storage fallback will not convert this input back to sparse NDArray and fail.

We should use temp storage when `fix_gamma == True` instead of changing input. Need to fix it for #7082

issue_title: Badly formatted string inputs cause ValueError instead of SwaggerMappingError
issue_body:
Given a `string` value that has a `date-time` format, an incorrectly formatted input (say, "string") will cause `dateutil.parser.parse` to raise `ValueError: Unknown string format` when unmarshalling the request.  This isn't checked anywhere, so it bubbles up as an unexpected error, rather than being reported as invalid input.

It seems like the place to check this is in `unmarshal_primitive`: the call to [formatter.to_python](/Yelp/bravado-core/blob/master/bravado_core/unmarshal.py#L72) could be wrapped in a try block, with `ValueError` trapped and re-raised as a `SwaggerMappingError` (allowing callers to recognize this as a malformed request)—is that the right fix?

issue_title: Format double and overriding default formats
issue_body:
I have a use case that is using double numbers in the spec for monetary values.  Given that float is so imprecise, it would seem more valid to me to cast these to Decimal() from the decimal module.

btw, DEFAULT_FORMATS specifies both 'double' and 'float' format to the float()

Currently bravado-core does not allow you to override the DEFAULT_FORMATS, of course I can rename my spec format to decimal and get this to work for my implementation.  But I guess this brings up two questions:

1) Should you be able to override the DEFAULT_FORMATS in practice?
2) Should perhaps the default for double be to use the Decimal() class?

Thanks for sharing this awesome module.

issue_title: Config for allowing .scss files with dependencies
issue_body:
[This issue](https://github.com/django-compressor/django-compressor/issues/30#issuecomment-220477807) was posted in django-compressor. I'm not sure which library would be best to provide this support.

The problem is that I can't use a `variables.scss` separate from other dependencies.
I've got around this by using a `main.css` and lots of `@imports` in there, which works just fine and is clean enough to me, but django-compressor doesn't notice when files other than `main.scss` are changed, requiring a server-restart for any scss that were `@imported`.

Webassets [solved this](https://github.com/miracle2k/webassets/blob/master/src/webassets/filter/sass.py#L192) by setting a `SASS_AS_OUTPUT` flag that concats files before processing them.

Do you think it's best solved in django-compressor or here?

issue_title: Alternative storage and verification scheme for client Public keys with CURVE authentication
issue_body:
Hi

I am working on a project using CURVE authentication. Clients obtain the keys at startup via HTTPS API calls, so that they are not stored permanently on remote devices.  For these reasons client keys are stored in a db and are copied to files in a credentials folder accessible to the pyzmq Authenticator. 
Every time a key is added to/removed from the db we have to modify the content of the  credentials folder accordingly and call configure_curve to update the state of the Authenticator.  

I think it would be useful to provide an alternative scheme where during ZAP authentication, a callback is called to asses the client public key validity.

I will try to provide a pull request for this.

issue_title: Out-of-order message and slow performance when closing and reconnecting socket
issue_body:
I have some code that would try to send a message, call close() and potentially reconnect again like the following:

```python
import threading

_NUM_MESSAGE = 1000


class ReconnectThread(threading.Thread):
    def __init__(self, ctx, server_url):
        super(ReconnectThread, self).__init__()
        self.ctx = ctx
        self.server_url = server_url

    def _get_new_socket(self):
        socket = self.ctx.socket(zmq.DEALER)
        socket.linger = 1000
        socket.connect(self.server_url)
        return socket

    def run(self):
        for i in range(_NUM_MESSAGE):
            sock = self._get_new_socket()
            sock.send_multipart([('blah_%s' % i).encode('utf8')])
            sock.close()


if __name__ == "__main__":
    import zmq
    ctx = zmq.Context()
    try:
        server_socket = ctx.socket(zmq.DEALER)
        server_url = 'tcp://127.0.0.1:7384'
        server_socket.bind(server_url)
        thread = ReconnectThread(ctx, server_url)
        thread.start()
        for i in range(_NUM_MESSAGE):
            print(server_socket.recv_multipart())
    finally:
        ctx.destroy()
```
First of all, I'd expect the message to be appear in order, but I'd get something like this instead:

```
[b'blah_0']
[b'blah_1']
[b'blah_3']
[b'blah_10']
[b'blah_7']
[b'blah_8']
...
```
Second, it seems like when linger is set to 1000 (1 second), it's not enough and the program would hang (since the message seems to be dropped).  I'd expect one second would be sufficient to send one (or even 1000 messages).  When I run this with a longer linger (say 5000 or 10000), it would terminate correctly, but the output sometimes would stuttered for a few seconds.

To me the behavior seems somewhat strange.  Does anybody explain to me why?  It makes the close() unreliable since I always set linger to be reasonable (to prevent the close() from hanging when there's message in the queue) and it's hard to set it correctly when this is happening.

I'm using pyzmq 17.0.0 with libzmq 4.2.5 running on Linux Python 3.6.5.

issue_title: option to collapse fully-sanitized lines when cleaning
issue_body:
(this is an idea brought up for discussion)

Bleach sanitizes content in-place, which is the ideal behavior for most situations, allowing a "line for line" comparison of input and output.

In certain situations, it may be desirable to omit those lines which become "empty" because of sanitization

    >>> line = "one
two
<!-- foo -->
three

four"
    >>> bleach.clean(line)
    u'one
two

three

four'

In the case above, a user might want the output to not include the `

` between "two" and "three" as it was caused by a stripped node occurring both after & before a newline.

    u'one
two
three

four'

issue_title: Session.run() method DOES NOT run a cypher statement within an auto-commit transaction.
issue_body:
Hi neo4j-python-driver developers, 

I was using `BoltSession` and found that its `run()` method just posts two messages, `RUN` and `PULL-ALL`, to its connection message queue, not exactly functions as [your document](https://neo4j.com/docs/api/python-driver/current/session.html#neo4j.v1.Session.run) says:

> Run a Cypher statement within an auto-commit transaction.

This is really misleading since I have a long query which may not finish before my script quits. It's better to revise your document and recommend other users to use the following practice:

```python
with session.begin_transaction():
    tx.run("query")
```

Thanks!
Yao

issue_title: Single worker behavior considered dangerous
issue_body:
As [discussed](https://groups.google.com/forum/#!topic/luigi-user/mH7NWfOr-4I) in luigi-user@ email group.

When running a single worker, luigi runs the heartbeat thread in the same process as the task itself. Blocking method calls (like blocking IO) happening from the task code prevent heartbeats from being sent by the heartbeat thread.

That makes locks obtained by that worker time out, and:
* pending tasks spuriously do not get run (in case when there are no redundant workers working on the same workflow), or
* duplicates get run (in the case when there are other workers and they meanwhile manage to reschedule and claim a task forgotten by scheduler that is already in progress).

When running several workers, luigi runs the heartbeat thread in a separate process, and the above problem does not occur.

It would be preferable to not have such astonishing special considerations linked to worker count.

issue_title: GCSClient.download()/.put() is unusably slow for large files
issue_body:
**TL;DR**: It takes 20 _MINUTES_ to download a `1.7 GB` files with `GCSClient()` but `gsutil` does it in about 20 seconds.  Working on a PR to parameterize `chunk_size` and suggest the default be changed to `10 MB`.  Not sure why the Google docs recommend such a small chunk size.

`GCSClient.download()` downloads files in [`1 MB` chunks](https://github.com/spotify/luigi/blob/672856d3b01428681c390773f5e15346c49f3ec5/luigi/contrib/gcs.py#L336), which is what the [Google Cloud Storage API docs use](https://cloud.google.com/storage/docs/json_api/v1/objects/get).  Select `python` from the menu under `Examples`.  `GCSClient.put()` uses a [`2 MB`](https://github.com/spotify/luigi/blob/672856d3b01428681c390773f5e15346c49f3ec5/luigi/contrib/gcs.py#L245) [chunk size](https://github.com/spotify/luigi/blob/672856d3b01428681c390773f5e15346c49f3ec5/luigi/contrib/gcs.py#L55).

Obviously this works but is incredibly slow for large files as each API call costs about `0.5` seconds.  I ran some download tests against `gsutil` on a [`n1-standard-2`](https://cloud.google.com/compute/docs/machine-types) machine with a `50GB` boot disk in zone `us-central-a` with a file that is `1.7 GB`.  Did not test upload but it appears to be about as slow.

| Chunk Size | Time (m:s) |
|:---------------:|:-------:|
| gsutil | 00:21 |
| 1 mb | 20:04 |
| 10 mb | 01:01 |
| 100 mb | 00:44 |

Test code:

```python
import json
import httplib2
import oauth2client.client

from googleapiclient import errors
from googleapiclient import discovery
from googleapiclient import http
import io
from apiclient.http import MediaIoBaseDownload

oauth_credentials = oauth2client.client.GoogleCredentials.get_application_default()

client = discovery.build('storage', 'v1', credentials=oauth_credentials, http=httplib2.Http())

bucket_name='{bucket}'
object_name='2015-01-01.nmea'

# Get Metadata
req = client.objects().get(
        bucket=bucket_name,
        object=object_name)
resp = req.execute()
#print json.dumps(resp, indent=2)

# Get Payload Data
req = client.objects().get_media(
        bucket=bucket_name,
        object=object_name)
# The BytesIO object may be replaced with any io.Base instance.
fh = io.FileIO(object_name, 'w')
downloader = MediaIoBaseDownload(fh, req, chunksize=10*1024*1024)
done = False
while not done:
    status, done = downloader.next_chunk()
```

issue_title: Make plugins "more optional"
issue_body:
Right now, although it's possible to turn off various plugins (e.g. pydocstyle, rope completions etc.) the pydocstyle and rope dependencies are still required.

issue_title: The event based risk calculator is using too much memory
issue_body:
Catalina is sending the Zurich cluster out of memory. The problem is that we are keeping all the IMTs and all the realizations in memory at the same time when computing the GMFs. In a SARA model with 54 realizations and 3 IMTs it means that we are using 54 x 3 = 162 times more memory than needed.
We should change the calculator to keep in memory only one IMT and realization at the time. This looks hard to implement but possible.

issue_title: DatabaseScheduler sync problem
issue_body:
In DatabaseScheduler in sync function:
` def sync(self):
        info('Writing entries...')
        _tried = set()
        try:
            with transaction.atomic():
                while self._dirty:
                    try:
                        name = self._dirty.pop()
                        _tried.add(name)
                        self.schedule[name].save()
                    except (KeyError, ObjectDoesNotExist):
                        pass
        except DatabaseError as exc:
            # retry later
            self._dirty |= _tried
            logger.exception('Database error while sync: %r', exc)`

Property self.schedule is never called If self._dirty is empty.
Which means that celery beat doesn't check if there are any changes in database. 

I solved it by adding self.schedule at the end of sync function. 
I think it's not the best solution. At least quite confusing.

issue_title: Operations/methods missing paginators
issue_body:
I brought this up at the openspace at pycon, there are lots of client methods missing paginator metadata. I went ahead and coded up simple script to identify all the missing paginators in botocore json sdk metadata.

```python
import boto3
import yaml

def main():
    session = boto3.Session()
    services = session.get_available_services()
    op_missing_paginator = {}
    for s in services:
        c = session.client(s)
        op_to_py = {v: k for k, v in c._PY_TO_OP_NAME.items()}
        for op in c.meta.service_model.operation_names:
            opm = c.meta.service_model.operation_model(op)
            if opm.output_shape is None:
                continue
            if 'NextToken' not in opm.output_shape.members:
                continue
            py_name = op_to_py[opm.name]
            if not c.can_paginate(py_name):

issue_title: s3 client cannot head or get objection versions for delete markers
issue_body:
The s3 api returns a 405 status code for HEAD and GET requests using a versionId for a delete marker, which causes botocore to raise a `ClientError` (in `BaseClient._make_api_call`).

The docs indicate that both `head_object` and `get_object` should return a `dict` with `DeleteMarker: True` when the requested `VersionId` is for a delete marker.

This seems like it could actually be a bug in S3, which should probably not respond with a 405 for either method when a `versionId` is supplied.

issue_title: Orphan affiliations
issue_body:
Some ~20K records:
https://inspirehep.net/search?ln=en&p=902%3A**&of=hb&action_search=Search&sf=earliestdate&so=d&wl=0

have orphan affiliations, i.e. affiliation not attached to a specific author but just available for searching.

This are stored in MARC 902__a for Literature. We should preserve this field, because currently, it's not possible to recompute the affiliation for many of the affected records due to missing PDF.

issue_title: Inconsistent redirect behavior between browsers
issue_body:
I'm using splinter with Lettuce, more or less as outlined in [this post](http://cilliano.com/2011/02/07/django-bdd-with-lettuce-and-splinter.html).

I am using the following code to call a django view that returns a `301`:

`world.browser.visit(django_url('/url_that_returns_a_301/'))`

And then asserting:

`assert world.browser.status_code.code == 301`

If I use the `firefox` webdriver (`world.browser = Browser('firefox')`) the status code is indeed `301`, however if I change the browser to django (`world.browser = Browser('django')`) the status code is `200`, because the redirect has been followed.

This is clearly because the django driver is doing:

`self._response = self._browser.get(url, follow=True)`

However, I would expect a compatible API between drivers.

Can I suggest one or both of the following:

1. Removing the `follow=True` form the django driver to make it act more like firefox.
2. Add an optional argument `follow` to `visit`, giving the tester the control over this.

I would be happy to do the latter in a PR, but I'd like to know if there is a reason for this.

2018-02-07 14:05:51.560:     - be included in your $PATH
2018-02-07 14:05:51.560:     - be set via $GIT_PYTHON_GIT_EXECUTABLE
2018-02-07 14:05:51.560:     - explicitly set via git.refresh()
```

After debugging the issue a bit, the problem was coming from the Popen call in Git.execute() -  throwing Exception '[WinError 6] The handle is invalid'

The issue / workaround is described here ([see stackoverflow link](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/40108816/python-running-as-windows-service-oserror-winerror-6-the-handle-is-invalid)) - subprocess throws exception where it fails to find std-in handle with argument stdin=None to Popen().
Using the same work around from the link corrected my issue running as a Windows Service:

The work-around, was to add something like the stdout_sink for stdin (see below) prior to calling Popen() within Git.execute():

```
  log.debug("Popen(%s, cwd=%s, universal_newlines=%s, shell=%s)",
                  command, cwd, universal_newlines, shell)
  + if( istream is None):
  +          istream = subprocess.DEVNULL
  try:
            proc = Popen(command,
```

Not sure this if is the best solution, but it allows me to use GitPython running as Windows Service.

issue_title: The --venv command-line option is confusingly named, shouldn't it be --virtualenv or --env-dir?
issue_body:
`pipenv` includes an option named `--venv` which outputs virtualenv information:

```
$ pipenv --venv
/home/example/.local/share/virtualenvs/bla-534BN
```

This is a confusingly named command-line option. `pipenv` does not use [`venv`](https://docs.python.org/3/library/venv.html) at all, it uses `virtualenv`, and yet this command-line option is named after `venv`, not `virtualenv`. Both `venv` and `virtualenv` play similar roles, and so it's confusing to get one named for the other.

I propose deprecating `--venv`, and introducing a better named flag, maybe `--virtualenv` or `--env-dir`.

issue_title: Same versions of Pipfile + Pipenv generate different Pipenv.lock
issue_body:
My coworker and I both have Pipenv 11.9.0, operating in a repo with the same Pipfile + lockfile (included below). When he runs `pipenv lock` the lockfile stays the same. When I run it it removes a bunch of packages (seemingly mostly deps of `django-debug-toolbar` in my `[dev-packages]` section). But clearly these deps are needed because when running the server I then get ImportErrors with some of the missing deps (namely `sqlparse`).

Any idea why `pipenv lock` would be producing different dependency trees here? This also occurs for any other task (like pipenv install/uninstall) that modifies the lockfile — on his it just installs/uninstalls what it's supposed to, on mine it removes a bunch of other things.

The only other difference is he is on python 3.6.5 while i am on 3.6.4.

------------

Pipenv behaves consistently accross our machines in resolving and installing dependencies

issue_title: `pipenv install` installs "develop" packages
issue_body:
Hello

On latest version of Pipenv (9.0.0), I see that `pipenv install` also install `develop` packages, it acts like `pipenv install --dev`.

Step to reproduce:
- clean directory
- `pipenv install --dev pylint`
- `pipenv --rm`
- `pipenv install` => we see that pylint is installed.

It does not appear when we skip the lock file:
- `pipenv install --skip-lock` => pylint is NOT installed

issue_title: Distutils does not detect library path correctly on win32
issue_body: On win32, the version of distutils doesn't quite work correctly, resulting in failures [like this](https://ci.appveyor.com/project/jaraco/setuptools/build/1.0.1227/job/r2aw9crxg44thxmp). It wasn't previously detected because tests weren't added until now.

issue_title: time to tag a release?
issue_body:
The last one was more than a year ago, in November 2016.
Lots of bugs have been fixed, including this one about PYTHON3.DLL not being copied to the venv's Scripts folder on Windows (https://github.com/pypa/virtualenv/pull/1083).

cffi recently added support for Py_LIMITED_API so all extension modules compiled with latest cffi links with PYTHON3.DLL and import fails from within a virtualenv.

https://bitbucket.org/cffi/cffi/issues/355/importerror-dll-load-failed-on-windows

cc @reaperhulk

issue_title: `PREFER_DATES_FROM` setting doesn't take into account parsing ambiguous years
issue_body:
It seems like the `PREFER_DATES_FROM` setting doesn't take into account parsing ambiguous years. 

```
zac@testv-dev ~/website/counsyl/product $ python
Python 2.7.3 (default, Jun 22 2015, 19:33:41) 
[GCC 4.6.3] on linux2
Type "help", "copyright", "credits" or "license" for more information.
>>> import dateparser
>>> dateparser.parse("10/10/49")
datetime.datetime(2049, 10, 10, 0, 0)
>>> dateparser.parse("10/10/49", settings={"PREFER_DATES_FROM": 'past'})
datetime.datetime(2049, 10, 10, 0, 0)
```

Even with `settings.PREFER_DATES_FROM = 'past'` it returns a `datetime` in the future

Seemingly relevant piece of code https://github.com/scrapinghub/dateparser/blob/1c35910b0649d191741942b3c1fba1a606610e56/dateparser/parser.py#L376-L393

issue_title: [question, but maybe problem?] is there any way to force the broker to use the same port used when accessing kafka?
issue_body:
# versions
* `kafka 0.11.0.1`
* `pykafka 2.7.0`

# context
* running kafka (among other services) in a `docker-compose` cluster locally
* running kafka tests on the host machine, which means I'm using host ports mapped to internal container ports to get access to the container services
* ports for services are exposed at _random_ host ports
    * this is done by telling `docker-compose` which ports to expose for which container, but not telling it _which_ host ports to map to. in that case, `docker-compose` finds open ports on the system and maps the exposed internal ports to those.
    * e.g. kafka may use port `9092` inside the container, but be randomly mapped to `31953` on the host
* interested in querying the offset information for various topics with commands such as `pykafka.topic.Topic.latest_available_offsets()

# problem/experience
`pykafka.KafkaClient()` connects to kafka at the random port **just fine**, but always uses port `9092` when attempting to connect to the broker, which doesn't work because `9092` is only valid inside the container.

## example connection pseudocode
```py
from pykafka import KafkaClient

kafka_port = determine_random_kafka_port()
kclient = KafkaClient(hosts='127.0.0.1:{}'.format(kafka_port))
latest_offsets = kclient.topics['topic-name'].latest_available_offsets()
```

# question
as the title says, is there any way to get the broker connection to use the same port that's provided to `pykafka.KafkaClient()` in the `hosts` parameter?

issue_title: json.dumps Fails when no indent option is provided
issue_body:
Calling `json.dumps` on a Customer object causes a TypeError when no `indent` option is provided. This is strange since the call does not raise an exception when an indent value _is_ provided. Here's an example:

```python
    import json

    stripe.Charge.create(...)

    # Works!
    customer_json = json.dumps(dict(customer), indent=2)

    # Raises `TypeError`
    customer_json = json.dumps(dict(customer))
```

I'm currently using Python 2.7.11, however, this may be an issue with the json library.

issue_title: DateEncoder only has 1 holiday
issue_body:
The dateencoder has functionality to indicate if a day is a holiday: 

https://github.com/numenta/nupic/blob/master/src/nupic/encoders/date.py#L297-L323

The problem is that it only lists December 25 as a holiday. You should probably be able to pass in your own holidays given that different countries have different holidays that may fall in different days of the year.

My proposal is to have an extra parameter you can pass in to a date encoder that is a list of named tuples that look like:
````
holidays = [holiday(year=2017, month=12, day=25), holiday(year=2018, month=4, day=1)]
````

If year isn't provided, it just uses the current year, otherwise it checks if year, month, day match the current record. This allows December 25, but also covers moving holidays like Easter Sunday (April 1 for 2018, April 16 for 2017)

issue_title: KNNClassifier wishlist
issue_body:
Improvements to KNNClassifier

- [ ] infer() should have options for sparse and dense inputs. Right now, for example, if distance metric is 'rawOverlap' infer must be called with a dense input even if classifier was trained on sparse input repr. If sparse input is used, the classification is non-deterministic
- [ ] Add getPatternCount() accessor method, possible others for a nice API
- [ ] Understand `partitionId` and implement, remove, or document appropriately
- [ ] Understand and then remove or document `rowID`
- [ ] Move `leaveOneOutTest` method from KNNClassifier module to appropriate test module
- [x] Improve explanations of constructor parameters
- [ ] Remove unused arguments from infer() method
- [ ] Move `leaveOneOutTest` to another module
- [ ] SVD / PCA features need better docs
- [ ] reorder parameters in constructor putting dependent parameters after their dependencies


There are several instances of KNN Classifier that one can make by specifying the following constructor arguments and, ideally, we should have adequate test coverage for the different possibilities:
- [ ] useSparseMemory (boolean)

distanceMethod (string)
- [ ] "norm"
- [ ] "rawOverlap"
- [ ] "pctOverlapOfLarger"
- [ ] "pctOverlapOfProto"


Ensure that infer() & learn() respond nicely to:
- [x] Sparse representation with unsorted indices 
- [x] Empty input list

issue_title: 503 if you post duplicate permissions

issue_title: es: wildcard support
issue_body:
Currently, our typeahead fields use [the wildcard notation](https://github.com/inspirehep/inspire-next/blob/master/inspire/modules/deposit/static/js/deposit/affiliations_typeahead.js#L33) in order to find a substring match in a given field.

While trying to replicate the behaviour with ES I have come to the same problem as http://stackoverflow.com/questions/23243867/elasticsearch-find-substring-match which can be solved by using [wildcard queries](https://www.elastic.co/guide/en/elasticsearch/reference/current/query-dsl-wildcard-query.html).

The current query parser, with a query like `*SLAC*` will send the following to ES:

```
u'query': {'bool': 
  {'must': [{'multi_match': {'query': u'*SLAC*', 'fields': ['experimentautocomplete']}},
  {'term': {u'_collections': u'Experiments'}}]}}
```

I don't think the query parser is able at the moment to recognize wildcards? This should be added to https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio-query-parser/ and to Invenio's ES walker.

issue_title: RFC table name standardisation
issue_body:
The Invenio-Records module tables bear a "traditional" name in singular, i.e. `record`. It may be good to profit from migration to Invenio v3.0 in order to standardise table names and module names globally.  E.g. the currently created Invenio v3.0 tables are:

```
invenio3=# \dt 
               List of relations
 Schema |       Name        | Type  |  Owner
--------+-------------------+-------+----------
 public | accounts_role     | table | invenio3
 public | accounts_user     | table | invenio3
 public | accounts_userrole | table | invenio3
 public | pidLOG            | table | invenio3
 public | pidSTORE          | table | invenio3
 public | record            | table | invenio3
 public | record_version    | table | invenio3
 public | transaction       | table | invenio3
(8 rows)
```

Note that pidSTORE and pidLOG is better to be expressed in all lowercase for easy PostgreSQL compliance. This seems to be already in the target of https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio-pidstore/pull/31

Note "accounts" but "record".

Note "accounts" but "user".

Proposed new convention: `tablename = modulename_*` which would call for using `records` and `editor` prefixes for tables, to take two examples.  This would align table naming convention to our [our module naming convention](http://pythonhosted.org/invenio/architecture/module-anatomy-naming.html).

CC @jirikuncar @lnielsen @nharraud

issue_title: errors: more specific error messages
issue_body:
Records-REST uses Flask's ``abort`` method to signal errors. E.g.:

```python
abort(415)
```

Invenio-REST will convert this into a response to the client which looks like this:

```http
HTTP/1.0 406 NOT ACCEPTABLE
Content-Type: application/json
Server: Werkzeug/0.11.3 Python/3.5.0
Date: Mon, 08 Feb 2016 13:35:48 GMT

{
  "message": "The resource identified by the request <...>.",
  "status": 406
}
```

The message is a generic error message which doesn't help client to figure out what they did wrong. Instead we should as much as possible provide proper error messages as to why a specific request went wrong.

This task is to review all ``abort`` calls, to check if a better error message can be provided. ``MaxResultWindowRESTError`` provides an example of how better error message can be provided.

issue_title: Update mapping when creating new message ID from existing one
issue_body:
In https://bugs.launchpad.net/zope.i18n/+bug/668189, @icemac reported:

> Creating new message IDs from existing message IDs should behave the following way:

```python
police_and_dog = _('${a} + ${b} = ${c}', mapping=dict(a='police', c='police dog'))
police_dog = _(police_and_dog, mapping=dict(b='dog'))
```

> Currently the mapping for `police_dog` is ignored but I think it should update the mapping of `police_and_dog` in the new message ID.
> 
> Currently you have to do the following to achieve the same:

```python
mapping = police_and_dog.mapping.copy()
mapping.update(dict(b='dog'))
police_dog = _(unicode(police_and_dog), mapping=mapping)
```

> Translating (zope.i18n.translate) should behave the same way.

issue_title: handling of compile_restricted optional params ``flags`` and ``dont_inherit``
issue_body:
@stephan-hof did bring up the point that the current handling of `flags` and `dont_inherit` did not work as expected in https://github.com/zopefoundation/RestrictedPython/pull/39#issuecomment-283074699

> Currently this inheritance is not compatible.
> Let's assume the following code in module called test.py
> 
```python 
from __future__ import print_function
from RestrictedPython import compile_restricted as compile
compile(<source>, filename="<inline>", mode="exec", ...) 
```
> Even if dont_inhert=False the future is not recognized in the compile_restricted function.
> When compile_restricted is called it uses the futures from compile.py not from test.py
> See: https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/2.7/Python/ceval.c#L4159
>
> If this inheriting should be compatible with the original compile function something like this needs to be done.
```python
parent_flags = sys._getframe(1).f_code.co_flags
parent_flags = parent_flags & PyCF_MASK
flags = flags | parent_flags
```
> and then call compile with flags=flags and dont_inhert=True to avoid using future flags from compile.py.
>
> Right now the users of RestrictedPython don't need this feature of future inheriting so I would leave it out. Typically the <source> comes from a different component anyway so inheriting the flags where restricted_compile is called could lead to surprising effects.

issue_title: Drop support for Django < 1.11
issue_body:
With this baitclick title I'd like to start a discussion on when we should drop the support for Django version less than 1.11. My first and most realistic proposition is to wait until 1.8 extended supported is dropped to ensure that everyone has time to update, however maybe we should speed it up slightly e.g. few months before 1.8 death? Everyone that cares about having latest and greatest versions will probably want to upgrade Django to 1.11.x LTS as well anyway. 

Obviously, even when we drop support for particular Django versions everyone will still be able to use versions of djoser compatible with these versions - it's just not recommended to do so, because one does not receive any fixes that way.

As to why it would be convenient - supporting multiple Django versions makes development slower, we could drop few compatibility tweaks, simplify tests, speed up CI etc.

issue_title: Failover is unsettable

There should be a supported way to set this value to `True`.

issue_title: [revamp] p4a silently fails if --private is not absolute
issue_body:
This one tripped me up for a while, because the documentation (both master and revamp) was not clear on where the entry point to the Python code should go. But eventually I discovered that if `--private` is given a relative path (on Linux), it silently fails: `assets/private.mp3` is successfully built without any of the app code, resulting in an APK that installs but doesn't start.

Either `--private` should be extended to support relative paths, or the documentation should say they aren't supported, and `p4a` should fail loudly.

issue_title: Numpy recipe hardcodes arm
issue_body:
Numpy's ar.patch hardcodes the use of the arm ar binary in the android tools, when it should use arch.compiler_prefix to choose the right one. This means it will probably not work on anything but arm, though it should be very simple to fix.

The compiler_prefix could be passed to numpy with e.g. an environment variable.

issue_body:
I am using PySDL2 + python-for-android *without kivy*. In my test app, the first orientation change seems to cause an `SDL_QUIT` and an `SDL_APP_TERMINATING` event, and I don't know why - it looks like a bug to me.

This is the test code (feel free to build & test for yourself, needs `--requirements=sdl2,pysdl2,python3crystax`):
```python3
#!/usr/bin/env python3

def apply_hack():
    import ctypes.util
    orig_func = ctypes.util.find_library
    def android_find_library_hack(*args):
        import os
        name = args[0]

        # Truncate ending for easier comparison:
        if name.find(".so.") >= 0:
            name = name.partition(".so.")[0]
        if name.endswith(".so"):
            name = name[:-len(".so")]
        if not name.endswith("."):
            name += "."

        # Helper function to check lib name:
        def check_name(lib_name, search_name):
            if filename.endswith('.so') and (
                    filename.startswith("lib" + search_name) or
                    filename.startswith(search_name)):
                return True
            if search_name.endswith("-2.0."):  # PySDL2 hack
                search_name = search_name[:-len("-2.0.")] + "."
                return check_name(lib_name, search_name)
            return False

        # Check the user app lib dir and system dir:
        app_root = os.path.normpath(os.path.abspath(os.path.join(
            os.path.dirname(__file__), '..', '..', 'lib')))
        sys_dir = "/system/lib"
        for lib_search in [app_root, sys_dir]:
            if not os.path.exists(lib_search):
                continue
            for filename in os.listdir(lib_search):
                if check_name(filename, name):
                    return os.path.join(lib_search, filename)
        return orig_func(*args)
    import ctypes.util
    ctypes.util.find_library = android_find_library_hack
apply_hack()

def handle_event(event):

    elif event.type == sdl.SDL_WINDOWEVENT:

                sdl.SDL_WINDOWEVENT_CLOSE:
            # Throw away renderer:

                sdl.SDL_DestroyRenderer(renderer)
                renderer = None

            # Close window:
            if window != None:
                sdl.SDL_DestroyWindow(window)
                window = None
                print("Window destroyed (window closed).")
                # Terminate if not on android:
                if sdl.SDL_GetPlatform().decode("utf-8").\
                        lower() != "android":
                    print("Terminating app since on desktop OS, " +
                        "and last window was closed.")
                    sys.exit(0)
        if (event.window.event ==
                sdl.SDL_WINDOWEVENT_RESTORED or

            # Re-create renderer if necessary:

issue_title: A better way to accommodate custom adb calls
issue_body:
This came up in a discussion with @winterfroststrom 

The `AdbProxy` was designed for the most straightforward use cases, like `adb.shell('getprop')` - simple call and return.

This does not work well if users want to have more control of the call.
E.g. process the live streaming of the stdout of the call subprocess.

We might want to consider providing a new adb interface for users who want to make long-running calls and have more control of the subprocess.

issue_title: Loading fcntl in terminal breaks windows load on Netmiko
issue_body:
I wanted to added textfsm parsing into the output of network device show commands in the Netmiko library, but the terminal.py module imports fcntl (which breaks Windows support):

https://github.com/google/textfsm/blob/master/terminal.py#L28

For use in TerminalSize() function. My use case doesn't use this TerminalSize() function.

Would you be willing to make that import conditional? 

For example, something like the following:

```
try:
    import fcntl
    USE_FCNTL = True
except ImportError:
    USE_FCNTL = False
``` 

Then on TerminalSize() something like:

```
def TerminalSize():
  """Returns terminal length and width as a tuple."""
  try:
    with open(os.ctermid(), 'r') as tty_instance:
      if USE_FCNTL:
          length_width = struct.unpack(
              'hh', fcntl.ioctl(tty_instance.fileno(), termios.TIOCGWINSZ, '1234'))
      else:
          # Basically raise an error so it falls to your other try/except handler
          raise OSError()
  except (IOError, OSError):
    try:
      length_width = (int(os.environ['LINES']),
                      int(os.environ['COLUMNS']))
    except (ValueError, KeyError):
      length_width = (24, 80)
  return length_width
```

If you are willing to accept a PR similar to this, I will write it and submit it.

issue_title: Allow parallel commands over ADB
issue_body:
Currently, one can only issue one command at a time. Issuing two or more will cause issues with the data being intermixed because the ADB client assumes a local-id of 1.

My current proposal is to alter the internal use of the `AdbMessage` class so that an instance is used, which holds the handle (also renamed to transport instead of usb to match android-adb). In the various `Connect` classmethods in `AdbCommands`, it will create a new instance of that class, but there will be a new method that accepts an existing instance. This will allow a new parallel version to be used, but only by folks interested in that, so we don't affect or slow existing users.

An entirely new class will be created for parallel connections, likely a thin wrapper around a dict of the `AdbMessage` replacement.

This will result in two things:
* Everything inside of `AdbCommands` will change rather drastically, so anyone using internal functions will get bit. We'll have to do a major revision change.
* Users who _didn't_ reach past `AdbCommands` and want parallel commands will have to make code changes to specify that, so they get the 'optimized' version that only allows a single stream.

The reason for allowing, and defaulting to, a single stream implementation is not as much for performance, as it is to reduce complexity in the normal case. The multi-stream version will have dispatching and lookups interleaved with everything a single-stream version has to do, making debugging more confusing. Users should be able to avoid multi-stream support when they don't need it, and be able to switch to single-stream when they're debugging something that may falsely show up as an adb issue.

issue_title: Not supporting @id values with relative URI reference value
issue_body:
On MacOS, using Python 2.7.5 with:

    rdflib==4.2.1
    rdflib-jsonld==0.3

The following file parses and returns the expected triples:

    { "@id":            "http://example.com/sampledata/testsite/_annalist_site/../"
    , "@base":          "http://example.com/sampledata/testsite/_annalist_site/./"
    , "@context":       "site_context.jsonld"
    , "rdfs:label":     "Annalist data notebook test site"
    , "rdfs:comment":   "Annalist test site metadata"
    , "annal:comment":  "Initialized from `sampledata/init/annalist_site/_annalist_site/site_meta.jsonld`"
    }

But this file parses OK but returns no triples:

    { "@id":            "../"
    , "@base":          "http://example.com/sampledata/testsite/_annalist_site/./"
    , "@context":       "site_context.jsonld"
    , "rdfs:label":     "Annalist data notebook test site"
    , "rdfs:comment":   "Annalist test site metadata"
    , "annal:comment":  "Initialized from `sampledata/init/annalist_site/_annalist_site/site_meta.jsonld`"
    }

The context file I'm using is this:

    {
      "@context": {
        "@base": ".",
        "annal": "http://purl.org/annalist/#",
        "owl": "http://www.w3.org/2002/07/owl#",
        "rdf": "http://www.w3.org/1999/02/22-rdf-syntax-ns#",
        "rdfs": "http://www.w3.org/2000/01/rdf-schema#",
        "xsd": "http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema#"
      }
    }

In case it's relevant, the code I'm using to do the parsing test looks like this:

        from rdflib import Graph
        g = Graph()
        s = self.testsite._read_stream()
        b = "file://" + os.path.join(TestBaseDir, layout.SITEDATA_DIR) + "/"
        result = g.parse(source=s, publicID=b, format="json-ld")
        print "***** g:"
        print(g.serialize(format='turtle', indent=4))

(The line `s = ...` returns a file-like object returning my purported JSON-LD data.)

This is important for me as I'm trying to create "portable" directory structures that can be interpreted as RDF using the current base URI of the data.  My reading of the JSON-LD spec is that relative references should be allowed for "@id" as long as there is an active base URI.  (I also want the @base to be relative to the document location, but that's a separate concern.)

issue_title: Use less RAM Integrating chromatic images
issue_body: @sowmyakth has been running into some RAM restrictions using ChromaticRealGalaxy on the SLAC clusters.  We identified at least one piece of galsim that could be improved in this respect, which is the way integ.py does the wavelength integral for chromatic drawImage and drawKImage.  Currently, the monochromatic images in the integral are first all drawn and put in memory, and then added together (with weights) at the end.  I think it's straight-forward to instead accumulate the integral as monochromatic images are drawn though, which would would have saved, in this particular case, around 1.5 GB of RAM.

issue_title: FA5 Documentation for NPM seems incorrect
issue_body:
Hi,

I have watched FA5 grow and evolve into the stunning icon library that it now is, but until now I haven't had a chance to use it for any projects (long and boring reasons). That being said, I have noticed that the NPM implementation doesn't work the way the documentation says it should. I have messed around with it for a while now and am pretty sure the implementation has changed without the docs being updated.

So here goes nothing... as per the docs we are expected to use something like the below.

```js
import fontawesome from '@fortawesome/fontawesome'

import {
  faFacebookF,
  faTwitter,
} from '@fortawesome/fontawesome-free-brands'

fontawesome.library.add(faFacebookF)
fontawesome.library.add(faTwitter)
```

This is all good and well but the `add` method only accepts an array of icons which is the implementation I've used below.

```js
import fontawesome from '@fortawesome/fontawesome'

import {
  faFacebookF,
  faTwitter,
} from '@fortawesome/fontawesome-free-brands'

fontawesome.library.add([
  faFacebookF,
  faTwitter,
])
```

My way works perfectly and I'll explain why. Using the documented method yields only one icon for use, from what I've seen while debugging the compiled code any new icon overwrites the last one in an endless cycle which isn't fun.

As mentioned, I've been testing this for quite a while now and am pretty sure it's a simple disconnect between the NPM package and the documentation. For reference, I'm using the below packages which are all up-to-date.

```
"@fortawesome/fontawesome": "^1.1.3"
"@fortawesome/fontawesome-free-brands": "^5.0.6"
"@fortawesome/fontawesome-pro-light": "^5.0.6"
"@fortawesome/fontawesome-pro-regular": "^5.0.6"
"@fortawesome/fontawesome-pro-solid": "^5.0.6"
```

Introduce OpenFisca command-line client

I'd like to introduce an OpenFisca command-line client.

issue_title: `cc.write` should update the schema of the final table to match dataframe
issue_body:
After Import API, use an inverted `pg2dtypes` to build an alter table query like this:
```SQL
ALTER TABLE {tablename}
ALTER COLUMN {col1} TYPE {newtype1} USING {col1}::{newtype1},
ALTER COLUMN {col2} TYPE {newtype2} USING {col2}::{newtype2},
...
```

This overrides the current default to just let CARTO guess the pgtype, and handles the less-than-desirable behavior of `encode_geom`'s now requiring us to have all columns as strings.

issue_title: large dataframes written with `lnglat=` keyword times out
issue_body:
A proposed solution is embedding the geometry piece into the queries that create the tables, like this:

```SQL
SELECT 'POINT ({lng} {lat})'::geometry(Point, 4326) as the_geom, {other_columns} ...
```
This avoids creating `the_geom` in the dataframe first and sending it, and it happens before the cartodbfytable operation, which does the indexing on `the_geom` and creates `the_geom_webmercator`. `UPDATES` on `the_geom` from cartodbfy'd tables might be slow because of triggers...

issue_title: better argparse sorting/description
issue_body:
Argparse arguments currently stay together based upon nested level, but their ordering within a nested level remain somewhat random.  I'd suggest that non-required arguments get pushed to the bottom, and Nested arguments get pushed to the top. 

so if i had

```Python
    import argschema

    class NestedSchema(argschema.schemas.DefaultSchema):
        aint = argschema.fields.Int(required=False, default =5, description="a integer")
        bstr = argschema.fields.Str(required=True, description = "a string")

    class MySchema(argschema.ArgSchema):
        nest = argschema.fields.Nested(NestedSchema, required=True, description="nested schema")
        topint = argschema.fields.Int(required=True,description="a top integer")
        topintb = argschema.fields.Int(default=5,required=False,description="a top integer b")
        topintc = argschema.fields.Int(required=True,description="a top integer c")
        topintd = argschema.fields.Int(default=7,required=False,description="a top integer d")

    if __name__ == '__main__':
        mod = argschema.ArgSchemaParser(schema_type=MySchema)
```

presently i get

```Shell
    $ python test_schema.py --help
    usage: test_schema.py [-h] [--log_level LOG_LEVEL] [--nest.aint NEST.AINT]
                        [--nest.bstr NEST.BSTR] [--input_json INPUT_JSON]
                        [--topintc TOPINTC] [--topintb TOPINTB]
                        [--topintd TOPINTD] [--topint TOPINT]
                        [--output_json OUTPUT_JSON]

    optional arguments:
    -h, --help            show this help message and exit
    --log_level LOG_LEVEL
                            set the logging level of the module
    --nest.aint NEST.AINT
                            a integer
    --nest.bstr NEST.BSTR
                            a string
    --input_json INPUT_JSON
                            file path of input json file
    --topintc TOPINTC     a top integer c
    --topintb TOPINTB     a top integer b
    --topintd TOPINTD     a top integer d
    --topint TOPINT       a top integer
    --output_json OUTPUT_JSON
                            file path to output json file
```
where I think i would prefer if the output were something like this

```Shell
    $ python test_schema.py --help
        usage: test_schema.py [-h] [--log_level LOG_LEVEL] [--nest.aint NEST.AINT]
                            [--nest.bstr NEST.BSTR] [--input_json INPUT_JSON]
                            [--topintc TOPINTC] [--topintb TOPINTB]
                            [--topintd TOPINTD] [--topint TOPINT]
                            [--output_json OUTPUT_JSON]

        optional arguments:                       
        --nest.aint NEST.AINT      a integer (default = 5)                        
        --nest.bstr NEST.BSTR      a string (required)       
        --topint TOPINT            a top integer (required)                   
        --topintc TOPINTC          a top integer c (required)
        --topintb TOPINTB          a top integer b (default=5)
        --topintd TOPINTD          a top integer d (default=7)
        --input_json INPUT_JSON    file path of input json file 
        --output_json OUTPUT_JSON  file path to output json file
        --log_level LOG_LEVEL      set the logging level of the module (default=WARNING)
        -h, --help                 show this help message and exit 
```

issue_title: Wrapped functions/classes cannot be well documented
issue_body:
PR #411 added the `requires` decorator which gives nice error message when an unmet optional dependency is encountered. It also strips the doc string off wrapped functions and class, for example; [read_odim_h5](http://arm-doe.github.io/pyart-docs-travis/user_reference/generated/pyart.aux_io.read_odim_h5.html#pyart.aux_io.read_odim_h5) and [RadarMapDisplay](http://arm-doe.github.io/pyart-docs-travis/user_reference/generated/pyart.graph.RadarMapDisplay.html#pyart.graph.RadarMapDisplay).  

The `__doc__` attribute can be copied unto the wrapped function using `functools.update_wrapper`, details in [Section 2.1 of SciPy-lecture](http://www.scipy-lectures.org/advanced/advanced_python/), but the function signature is still lost.  There are some [methods for preserving the function signature](http://stackoverflow.com/questions/147816/preserving-signatures-of-decorated-functions) but from what I read none of these work reliably across Python 2 and 3 without adding an additional dependency.  

I'm thinking of reverting back the PR since it seems to cause more harm than good but wanted to see if other have another solution.  The other option is to make a module like [decorator](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/decorator) a required dependency for Py-ART.

issue_title: _gate_to_grid_map module does not compile on Windows
issue_body:
The _gate_to_grid_map module does not compile on Windows as the Microsoft Visual Studio C++ compiler used to compile Python 2.7 modules does not implement the C99 isfinite function.  The current work around is to disable the check for finite field values.  

In addition Windows does not separate the math library from the standard C library so the "m" library is not need nor can be linked to the module.

issue_title: Test for unbounded flux
issue_body:
Purpose: A sizeable fraction of model reactions able to carry unlimited flux under default conditions indicates problems with reaction directionality, missing cofactors, incorrectly defined transport reactions and more. Some examples:

E. coli (iJO1366) ratio=0.05 (Orth et al.)
C. glutamicum (iJM658)	ratio=0.05 (Mei et al. Gene. 2016;575:615-22)
P.putida ratio=0.08 (Belda et al. Env Microbiol, 18(10), 2016, 3403-3424).
T. saccharolyticum ratio=0.19 (Currie et al. BMC Syst Biol. 2015; 9: 30.)
Spirulina Platensis (AK692) ratio=0.25 (Klanchui et al. BMC Systems Biology 2012; 6:71)


Procedure:

1. Perform flux variability analysis using the default model state and fraction_of_optimum=0.0 (could reuse results from find_blocked_reactions I guess)
2. Count number of reactions that are not blocked
3. Count the number of reactions with minFlux(i) ~ model.lb(i) or maxFlux(i) ~ model.ub(i) and divide by number of non-blocked reactions

issue_title: Issue with https and auth
issue_body:
So it's actually a couple of issues. The first one that when I use ntlm auth with https I keep getting 407

so I have:
session.proxies = {'http': 'http://ip:port', 'https': 'https://ip:port'}
session.auth = HttpNtlmAuth('domain\\user', 'password', session)

and when I try:
session.get('https://www.google.com') I get:
ConnectionError: HTTPSConnectionPool(host='www.google.com', port=443): Max retries exceeded with url: / (Caused by ProxyError('Cannot connect to proxy.', error('Tunnel connection failed: 407 Unauthorized',)))

I'm pretty sure this is due to the reason explained in here: https://lukasa.co.uk/2013/07/Python_Requests_And_Proxies/
and indeed, if I remove the https entry from the proxy list, I get a 200 response. Now, this only works when I turn the firewall off, otherwise I get:
ConnectionError: ('Connection aborted.', error(10013, 'An attempt was made to access a socket in a way forbidden by its access permissions'))
http is the only way I can get through the ntlm proxy with the firewall on

The real issue is when I try to use auth on a resource. Like I have a cloudant db and I use requests to query it, using session.auth to store the cloudant username and password. But if I want to use nltm auth session.auth is taken up by HttpNtlmAuth for the ntlm proxy so I can't authenticate into cloudant.

I've tried sending the cloudant creds as payload in the session data with no luck and I've looked as best as I could in the requests_ntlm code to see if there's any way of sending the credentials for the resource I'm trying to access, but again no luck.

issue_title: Mismatch between WriteLogEntriesRequest'
Enable / document BYO HTTP Objects
issue_body:
Was recently pointed out in https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/gcloud-python/issues/903#issue-83467800 that `httplib2.Http` objects are not threadsafe.

@mwitkow-io suggested that using [`urllib3`][1] connection pooling would be useful to them.

Other users may also want to use [Twisted][2] for their transport layer (instead of `httplib2`).

----

Currently (as of 9491053699708cef29cdbefeba65572e8481f067) this is possible in `datastore` because `Connection` takes a standalone `http` object (of any type the user pleases) and the [only place][3] the `http` object is used has a very clear signature

```python
headers, content = self.http.request(uri=..., method=..., headers=..., body=...)
```

----

Related to https://github.com/google/oauth2client/issues/128


[1]: https://urllib3.readthedocs.org/en/latest/
[2]: https://twistedmatrix.com/trac/
[3]: https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/gcloud-python/blob/3e86aaed3c7e192a17d9b790f90a41cc2b7baa6a/gcloud/datastore/connection.py#L84-L86

issue_body:
https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/blob/master/storage/google/cloud/storage/blob.py#L871
```
871:        self._set_properties(api_response['resource'])
```

This relies on the API to always return 'resource'.  However, it is optional as per the doc.
https://cloud.google.com/storage/docs/json_api/v1/objects/rewrite
"""
This property is present in the response only when copying completes.
"""

As such, when the write was partial, done: false is returned and resource not present.  Thus failing with the following KeyError.
```
Traceback (most recent call last): 
File "xxx.py", line 304, in test_011_CopyFileFromBucketToBucketNewName 
new_destination_file_name=new_file_name)

token, rewritten, total = destination_blob.rewrite(source_blob, token=token) 
File "/home/jenkins/venv/lib/python2.7/site-packages/google/cloud/storage/blob.py", line 681, in rewrite 
self._set_properties(api_response['resource']) 
KeyError: 'resource' 
```

The most easy fix would be to push _set_properties down under after done: false check:

```
E.g.;

       api_response = client._connection.api_request(
            method='POST', path=source.path + '/rewriteTo' + self.path,
            query_params=query_params, data=self._properties, headers=headers,
            _target_object=self)
        # self._set_properties(api_response['resource'])  ## Comment out this line
        rewritten = int(api_response['totalBytesRewritten'])
        size = int(api_response['objectSize'])

        if api_response['done']:
            return None, rewritten, size

        self._set_properties(api_response['resource']) # and move here 
        return api_response['rewriteToken'], rewritten, size
```

issue_title: Google cloud storage needs requests >= 2.18.0 but the setup.py only requires >= 2.4.0
issue_body:
According to the requests [changelog](http://docs.python-requests.org/en/latest/community/updates/) the Response class was turned into a context manager only in version 2.18.0. However in google/resumable_media/requests/download.py line 62 (inside _write_to_stream) the code tries to use "response" as a context manager. Some digging reveals that this response makes its way from Client._http which is defined in google/cloud/client.py and gets the Response object from google/auth/transport/requests.py:AuthorizedSession.

So... it definitely feels like something in here needs to require requests >= 2.18.0 (maybe all of them?). 

Thoughts?

Detailed info:

1. OS type and version: N/A
2. Python version and virtual environment information: 2.7 but I don't think it matters.
3. google-cloud-python version: google-cloud-storage v 1.3.1
4. Stacktrace if available: below.
5. Steps to reproduce: Install a version of the requests library _less than 2.18.0_. Get some blob that exists. Try blob.download_as_string(). I personally re-pro'd this with version 2.10.0 to check. Note that the setup.py only requires 2.0.0 for storage and 2.4.0 for client, etc...

-- PG.

issue_title: Monitoring: sending metrics in a non-blocking/buffered way
issue_body:
Hi there!

We're considering using this library to send metrics to Stackdriver monitoring.

I'm a bit concerned because the monitoring library seems to send metrics with a [synchronous HTTP connection](https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/blob/master/monitoring/google/cloud/monitoring/client.py#L66), thus coupling our services very tightly to Stackdriver and requiring a round-trip for each metric being sent.

In the the logging library, a [background thread](https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/blob/master/logging/google/cloud/logging/handlers/transports/background_thread.py#L17) seems to be used in order to buffer data locally before flushing it asynchronously.

Is there a built-in/suggested way to achieve a similar behaviour with metrics? I was considering subclassing the [Client](https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/blob/master/monitoring/google/cloud/monitoring/client.py#L52) class to do it.

issue_title: speech api with sample content bytes is not encoded to ASCII string before being sent over the wire
issue_body:
When using the package as under with python3.5 -

    with open('linear.wav', 'rb') as f:
        data = f.read()

    s = client.sample(data, None, encoding=Encoding.LINEAR16, sample_rate=16000)
    texts = client.sync_recognize(s)

Throws an error that it is not possible to convert the byte stream to JSON.

In `speech/google/cloud/speech/client.py#240` the following line encodes the bytes in base64 but the return type of b64encode is still bytes.

    audio = {'content': b64encode(_to_bytes(sample.content))}

If this is changed to the following, the API behaves as expected -

    audio = {'content': b64encode(_to_bytes(sample.content)).decode('ascii')}

issue_title: Spanner: Stable and Latest Documentation
issue_body:
I noticed that from #3740 
[GCP stable](https://googlecloudplatform.github.io/google-cloud-python/stable/spanner/transaction-usage.html)
which wasn't updated

but this was [GCP latest](https://googlecloudplatform.github.io/google-cloud-python/latest/spanner/transaction-usage.html)

Then there are also 
[RTD latest](https://google-cloud-python.readthedocs.io/en/latest/spanner/transaction-usage.html)
which apparently gets automatically updated

and
[RTD stable](https://google-cloud-python.readthedocs.io/en/latest/spanner/transaction-usage.html)

From talking with @dhermes it seems like the GCP doesn't get built automatically when builds break, but RTD bulids update automatically.  It seems redundant to have 4 branches when 2 will do.  And @dhermes also mentioned that there stable branches are not reachable by clicking.

It seems that we should have a stable branch that is easily found and pushed to automatically on build succeeds and a latest branch for currently developing documentation.

issue_title: PubSub JSON Double base64 Encode
issue_body:
We recently upgraded from 0.18.1 to 0.21.0 and we do *not* use gRPC/GAX/Protobuf fun times, but we use the JSONPublisherApi.

Maybe we are using the topic.Batch().Commit() method wrong, but we have been using it in our retry strategy to just simply call Commit() again.

With the change in #2577 it seems that Commit() will double encode *if* you just call Commit() again on a network failure.

Is this the intended design? Are we supposed to keep another copy of the message batch or create another message batch?

issue_title: User home directory mode unsafe
issue_body: In the [google_compute_engine/accounts/accounts_utils.py](https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/compute-image-packages/blob/e843cc6919bbed8203ef8ce87b5a3120d4e86800/google_compute_engine/accounts/accounts_utils.py#L173-L174) file on lines 173-174. Is the 755 permission required? Or can I make a PR setting it to something safer that will pass audits like 750?

issue_title: NVMe image is flawed
issue_body:
Hi there, sorry if this is the wrong place for this.

I notice that the special Debian NVMe image you have prepared has a custom kernel deb. It is versioned such that future backports from Debian will erase the NVMe patched kernel unless you pay attention to it. The best way to avoid this is to not use the same package name as the upstream one. Additionally, it would be nice to have the original deb available in a Debian repository somewhere, as well as the nvme patch itself so we can inspect its contents. I had to repack it myself from a different machine which had the same kernel because I mistakenly upgraded it.

If you did all of this, you probably wouldn't have to pin the package in /etc/apt/preferences.d.

I'm happy to help with this but am not sure if the source for this is tracked in github at all.

Thanks!

issue_title: Resolving pre-release when explicitly requested
issue_body:
So upgrading to pants 1.3.0 dev releases, I ran into an issue with an whl we have as a pre-release, pex is more picky with these than pip at the moment:

```
$ ls wheelhouse/pydicom-1.0.0a1-py2-none-any.whl 
wheelhouse/pydicom-1.0.0a1-py2-none-any.whl
$ pip install pydicom==1.0.0a1 -f wheelhouse/ --no-index
Collecting pydicom==1.0.0a1
Installing collected packages: pydicom
Successfully installed pydicom-1.0.0a1
$ pex pydicom==1.0.0a1 -f wheelhouse/ --no-index
Could not satisfy all requirements for pydicom==1.0.0a1:
    pydicom==1.0.0a1
```

Looking through pep 440 and pip code, it looks like if any specifier in a set is inclusive and specifies a pre-release, then pre-releases should be accepted.

It seems like that could just be a check here: https://github.com/pantsbuild/pex/blob/d70f2c4e679770a586c765eaaa5ce6466bbc8879/pex/package.py#L82 if operator is inclusive and version is prerelease?

issue_title: Expose a way to query whether a particular goal is clean/dirty
issue_body:
The `build_invalidator` stores a mapping from target id to target fingerprint, and so given a target and a fingerprint strategy, it can answer queries about whether a target needs to be rebuilt.

It would be useful to expose this as a task/goal for the intellij pants plugin to consume. Currently, the `./pants invalidate` goal purges this information, but nothing exposes it for a user... part of the reason for that might be the requirement to have the relevant instance of the task, and the fingerprint strategy for the class.

In the new engine this should get easier, because the query will simply be "is this root node currently valid"? cc @kwlzn

issue_title: Support Java source, target levels for a given java_library
issue_body:
We have projects in our repo that expect to use Java 8, others that expect to use Java 7.  The javac compiler supports -source and -target specifications.  I was thinking we should be able to specify this at the java_library level so that Pants can find incompatibilities and pass the right arguments (target, source, bootstrapclasspath) to the compiler.

Previous discussion:
https://groups.google.com/forum/#!topic/pants-devel/diCYoTn7OWQ

issue_title: test_build_local_python_distributions is broken on OSX
issue_body:
The first problem is that src/python/pants/fs/archive.py hard-codes liblzma's file extension as .so where it's .dylib on OSX.

After fixing that, src/python/pants/backend/native/subsystems/llvm.py uses `darwin` instead of `mac` as its _SYSTEM_ID key.

After that, I got
```
dyld: Library not loaded: /Users/stuhood/src/pantsbuild-binaries/build-support/bin/xz/mac/10.13/5.2.4/xz-5.2.4-osx/xz-install/lib/liblzma.5.dylib
                       Referenced from: /Users/dwagnerhall/.cache/pants/bin/xz/mac/10.13/5.2.4/xz/bin/xz
                       Reason: Incompatible library version: xz requires version 8.0.0 or later, but liblzma.5.dylib provides version 6.0.0
```

It appears that the `xz` binary on pants-binaries is hard-coded to look in @stuhood's homedir for its dynamic libraries.

```
$ ${HOME}/.cache/pants/bin/xz/mac/10.13/5.2.4/xz/bin/xz --version
dyld: Library not loaded: /Users/stuhood/src/pantsbuild-binaries/build-support/bin/xz/mac/10.13/5.2.4/xz-5.2.4-osx/xz-install/lib/liblzma.5.dylib
  Referenced from: /Users/dwagnerhall/.cache/pants/bin/xz/mac/10.13/5.2.4/xz/bin/xz
  Reason: Incompatible library version: xz requires version 8.0.0 or later, but liblzma.5.dylib provides version 6.0.0
Abort trap: 6 (core dumped)
```
Setting the search path makes the binary work:
```
$ DYLD_FALLBACK_LIBRARY_PATH=${HOME}/.cache/pants/bin/xz/mac/10.13/5.2.4/xz/lib ${HOME}/.cache/pants/bin/xz/mac/10.13/5.2.4/xz/bin/xz --version
xz (XZ Utils) 5.2.4
liblzma 5.2.4
```

Looks like we need to either be setting xz's library search path better when we link it (or in a wrapper script when we execute it), or statically linking it.
