issue_title: Base.__nonzero__ breaks Base.depth
issue_body:
I have a use case where I was checking the depth of a constraint. In Base._depth the following code is executed,

angr/claripy/ast/base.py
```python
389        for a in ast_args:
390            if a not in memoized:
```

If `a` is an AST with depth 1 and `memoized` contains an element whose key is another AST with depth 1, then `Base.__nonzero__` will eventually be called causing a `ClaripyOperationError` due to the truthness of the AST being tested. 

```
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/claripy/ast/base.py", line 377, in depth
    return self._depth()
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/claripy/ast/base.py", line 391, in _depth
    memoized[a] = a._depth(memoized)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/claripy/ast/base.py", line 391, in _depth
    memoized[a] = a._depth(memoized)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/claripy/ast/base.py", line 391, in _depth
    memoized[a] = a._depth(memoized)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/claripy/ast/base.py", line 391, in _depth
    memoized[a] = a._depth(memoized)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/claripy/ast/base.py", line 391, in _depth
    memoized[a] = a._depth(memoized)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/claripy/ast/base.py", line 391, in _depth
    memoized[a] = a._depth(memoized)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/claripy/ast/base.py", line 390, in _depth
    if a not in memoized:
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/claripy/ast/base.py", line 537, in __nonzero__
    raise ClaripyOperationError('testing Expressions for truthiness does not do what you want, as these expressions can be symbolic')
ClaripyOperationError: testing Expressions for truthiness does not do what you want, as these expressions can be symbolic
Uncaught exception. Entering post mortem debugging
Running 'cont' or 'step' will restart the program
> /usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/claripy/ast/base.py(537)__nonzero__()
-> raise ClaripyOperationError('testing Expressions for truthiness does not do what you want, as these expressions can be symbolic')
```
Since the python stack trace did not show a direct call to  `Base.__nonzero__` I traced python to see where the call was made. 
```c
Breakpoint 1, PyObject_IsTrue (v=v@entry=<Bool at remote 0x7f45238e7e90>)
    at ../Objects/object.c:1568
1568	in ../Objects/object.c
(gdb) info stack
#0  PyObject_IsTrue (v=v@entry=<Bool at remote 0x7f45238e7e90>)
    at ../Objects/object.c:1568
#1  0x0000000000562761 in PyObject_RichCompareBool (v=<optimized out>, 
    w=<optimized out>, op=<optimized out>) at ../Objects/object.c:1010
#2  0x0000000000562908 in lookdict.18241 (mp=0x7f452f5f86e0, 
    key=<optimized out>, hash=-4146926773084927565)
    at ../Objects/dictobject.c:342
#3  0x0000000000522dd7 in PyDict_Contains (
    op={<BV at remote 0x7f452f1a12a8>: 1, <BV at remote 0x7f4524bc12a8>: 2, <BV at remote 0x7f452e6da438>: 1, <BV at remote 0x7f452e914d98>: 1, <Bool at remote 0x7f4524ba2b90>: 3}, key=<optimized out>) at ../Objects/dictobject.c:2292
```

It appears that `PyObject_RichCompareBool` decides that if the object is not a bool then it will check to see if it is true, which results in the call to `Base.__nonzero__`.

Is `Base.__nonzero__` still necessary or can it be removed to fix this issue?

issue_title: VSA .add() API inconsistent
issue_body:
We discussed this somewhat on irc some weeks ago.

This is the test code:

```python
sv = claripy.FullFrontend(claripy.backends.z3)
x = claripy.BVS('x', 32)
sv.add(x < 50000) 
sv.add(x < 30000) 
sv.add(x > 20000)
print sv.max(x),"-",sv.min(x)

sv = claripy.FullFrontend(claripy.backends.z3)
x = claripy.BVS('x', 32)
sv.add([x < 50000, x < 30000, x > 20000])
print sv.max(x),"-",sv.min(x)
```
Which yields the following (correct) results:
```
29999 - 20001
29999 - 20001
```

If I replace the solver to vsa:
```python
sv = claripy.ReplacementFrontend(claripy.LightFrontend(claripy.backends.vsa), replace_constraints=True, complex_auto_replace=True)
x = claripy.BVS('x', 32)
sv.add(x < 50000) 
sv.add(x < 30000) 
sv.add(x > 20000)
print sv.max(x),"-",sv.min(x)

sv = claripy.ReplacementFrontend(claripy.LightFrontend(claripy.backends.vsa), replace_constraints=True, complex_auto_replace=True)
x = claripy.BVS('x', 32)
sv.add([x < 50000, x < 30000, x > 20000])
print sv.max(x),"-",sv.min(x)
```

Yields the following:
```
49999 - 0
29999 - 19999
```
It seems, that VSA only uses the first specified constraint (first `add` call), when calculating its result.

If we specify all the constraints in one `add` call, then it works, but it miscalculates the UGT(>) expression (probably when inverting it).

issue_title: satellite.get_lonlatalt(now) returns wrong longitude with numpy < 1.11
issue_body:
[numpy documentation regarding this behaviour](https://docs.scipy.org/doc/numpy-1.14.0/reference/arrays.datetime.html#changes-with-numpy-1-11)

if users with numpy < 1.11 are using the latest version of pyorbital, the timezone on their device will be used to calculate ```np.datetime64('2000-01-01T12:00'))``` in ```jdays2000()``` in ```astronomy.py```. In numpy => 1.11, the ```np.datetime64``` will always be in UTC by default. 

Suggestions:
check ```np.__version__``` in ```jdays2000()``` if you want to support older versions of numpy or change the requirements to np=>1.11

issue_title: PCA: too much memory
issue_body:
In the manual (29.14.2. Cartesian covariance matrix calculation and projection (PCA)

```bash
# Step one. Generate average structure.
# RMS-Fit to first frame to remove global translation/rotation.
parm data/tz2.parm7
trajin data/tz2.nc
rms first !@H=
average crdset AVG
run
# Step two. RMS-Fit to average structure. Calculate covariance matrix.
# Save the fit coordinates.
rms ref AVG !@H=
matrix covar name MyMatrix !@H=
createcrd CRD1
run
# Step three. Diagonalize matrix.
runanalysis diagmatrix MyMatrix vecs 2 name MyEvecs
# Step four. Project saved fit coordinates along eigenvectors 1 and 2
crdaction CRD1 projection evecs MyEvecs !@H= out project.dat beg 1 end 2
```

The `createcrd CRD1` step used up my RAM. Is the `createcrd` idiom for `projection`?

issue_body: I've heard many Crawlera users complaining about installing the certificate (to make https work with Crawlera) and I was thinking that, since the Crawlera middleware is meant to make things easier on the client side, it could automatically download and install the certificate if it's not present, to reduce another step in the "crawlera setup process". issue_title: Expand GET/POST search tests
issue_body:
Pulp supports searching via both GET and POST requests. When searching with a GET request, parameters should be passed in via the URL, and when searching with a POST request, parameters should be passed in the body of the request. (This is appropriate behaviour, by the way. Network intermediaries are at liberty to discard the body of any GET request.) Unfortunately, this makes Pulp's search facilities more complicated and, therefore, more prone to bugs.

Tests should be written that execute identical GET and POST searches and verify that the results are identical. Much of the searching code is very generic and applies no matter what kind of entity is being searched for. As a result, even adding a few tests should help immensely with preventing regressions.

Further documentation on the GET and POST search queries is available [here](https://pulp.readthedocs.org/en/latest/dev-guide/conventions/criteria.html#search-api).

issue_title: Add totalCount field to connections
Argument of type `SomeEnum` does not map back to `SomeEnum.VALUE` when used as an argument
issue_body:
I couldn't figure out how to succinctly word the summary.

When receiving an Enum as an argument, it's not possible to compare it against the class that defined it.  That is, `args['episode'] == Episode.NEWHOPE` will be `False` on `query { foo(episode:NEWHOPE) }`.  This seems counter-intuitive

```python
class Episode(Enum):
    NEWHOPE = 4
    EMPIRE = 5
    JEDI = 6


class Query(ObjectType):
    foo = String(
        args={'blah': Episode(default_value=Episode.NEWHOPE)}
    )

    def resolve_foo(self, args, context, info):
        import sys

        blah = args.get('blah')
        return (
            "Value of blah is: {!r}\n"
            "(blah == NEWHOPE) is: {!r}\n"
            "Python version is: {!r}\n"
            ""
        ).format(
            blah,
            blah == Episode.NEWHOPE,
            sys.version
        )

```

```
query {
  foo(blah:NEWHOPE)
}
```

yields (de-JSOnified for easier reading):

```
Value of blah is: 4
(blah == NEWHOPE) is: False
Python version is: '3.5.2 (default, Nov 17 2016, 17:05:23) \\n[GCC 5.4.0 20160609]'
```


"Native" Enums don't compare to their 'actual' representation either...

```python
>>> import enum
>>> class Episode(enum.Enum):
...     NEWHOPE = 4
...
>>> Episode.NEWHOPE == 4
False
```
... but they can be converted; e.g. `Episode(4) == Episode.NEWHOPE`, which does not work in Graphene.   Additionally, Python's IntEnum class does permit straight comparisons to integer values.

The behavior here appears to contradict the documentation (emphasis mine):

> graphene.Enum uses enum.Enum internally (or a backport if that’s not available) *and can be used in the exact same way*.

IMHO, the correct behavior here would be for resolve_foo() to get `Episode.NEWHOPE` rather than `4` for the argument here, but this presumably would break a lot of existing code.  Perhaps some sort of option to accomodate this?  

Alternatively, having a `graphene.IntEnum` that's functionally equivalent to `enum.IntEnum` would be nice.

              ('Semi-Annually', 'Semi-Annually'),
              'Annually', 'Annually'))
        
        payment_frequency = models.CharField(
            blank=True,
            choices=PERIODIC_INTERVAL_CHOICES,
            max_length=13)

The schema generated by DjangoObjectType will show incorrectly show that this field `payment_frequency` is required.  This is incorrect -- the expected behavior is that it NOT required.

I have been able to fix this issue by patching how the value of required is determined in `graphene.converter.convert_django_field_with_choices` (shown for graphene-django 2.0.0)  :

    def convert_django_field_with_choices(field, registry=None):
        # Modified from graphene_django.converter import convert_django_field_with_choices
        # to adjust "required"
        choices = getattr(field, 'choices', None)
        if choices:
            meta = field.model._meta
            name = to_camel_case('{}_{}'.format(meta.object_name, field.name))
            choices = list(get_choices(choices))
            named_choices = [(c[0], c[1]) for c in choices]
            named_choices_descriptions = {c[0]: c[2] for c in choices}
    
            class EnumWithDescriptionsType(object):
    
                @property
                def description(self):
                    return named_choices_descriptions[self.name]
    
            enum = Enum(name, list(named_choices), type=EnumWithDescriptionsType)
            required = not (field.blank or field.null or field.default)  # MODIFIED FROM ORIGINAL
            return enum(description=field.help_text, required=required)
        return convert_django_field(field, registry)

issue_title: DjangoDebugMiddleware prevents exceptions from being logged
issue_body:
Hmm, I'm not sure what's going on, but I spent a very long time trying to figure out why exceptions in my GraphQL endpoint weren't making their way to my logging subsystem.

Note that by default, Graphene/graphql-core logs exceptions that occur to the logging subsystem:

```python
import graphene


class MyThing(graphene.ObjectType):
    foo = graphene.String()
    bar = graphene.String()

    def resolve_foo(self, info):
        return "hi"

    def resolve_bar(self, info):
        raise Exception('some weird unexpected error happened!')


schema = graphene.Schema(query=MyThing)

# This prints 'hi'.
print(schema.execute('query { foo }').data['foo'])

# This prints 'None' but first logs a helpful traceback.
print(schema.execute('query { bar }').data['bar'])
```

The second query logs the following:

```pytb
An error occurred while resolving field MyThing.bar
Traceback (most recent call last):
...
graphql.error.located_error.GraphQLLocatedError: some weird unexpected error happened!
```

However, no such logging message occurred when I bound my schema to a Graphene-Django `GraphQLView`, despite configuring my Django log settings properly.

I eventually figured out that if I sent my `settings.GRAPHENE["MIDDLEWARE"]` to `None`, the errors started being logged again.

It seems the default middleware used if I don't force it to `None` is `DjangoDebugMiddleware`, so perhaps this is doing something to prevent the errors from being logged?

issue_title: Using a specified database
issue_body:
Django provides the ability to query a particular database. For example, these are equivalent:

```python
User.objects.all()
User.objects.using('default')
```

For performance/scalability, we direct our reads to a secondary database. In this case we do `User.objects.using('secondary')` to read.

We'd like to be able to specify a default database to be used for queries.

Second to this, we'd also like to preserve the ["read your writes"](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Consistency_model#Read-your-writes_Consistency) property as much as we can by, in mutations, writing to and then reading from the primary/default database.

Essentially, something like this would be ideal:

```python
schema = graphene.Schema(
    query=Query,
    query_database='secondary',
    mutation_database='primary',
)
```

I'm not sure where is best to put this API, or whether it already exists (I can't find it in the docs or source), but the above would satisfy the requirements. There could well be a nicer place to specify this.

Ideally whatever the option is, it would allow for setting a global default per operation type, along with overrides per type, rather than needing to be explicitly specified per type.

Thoughts?

issue_title: system_requirements() and docker doesn't play well
issue_body:
conan package tools has a feature to run builds inside docker.
note that docker invocation always mounts conan cache folder, see https://github.com/conan-io/conan-package-tools/blob/e4be5af6d8a5fb34f3ffc7273166e44bf8b97ba2/conan/create_runner.py#L161
if recipe is using system_requirements() that causes problems.
e.g. first build runs in Docker container, and installs system requirements, leaving record in cache.
then second build runs in the fresh Docker container (which obviously has no dependencies installed), but conan thinks that requirements are installed, because of leftover in cache from the previous run.
/cc @lasote @memsharded

issue_title: Add lasote/conanclang39 capability
Consider more advanced "build policy" commit message parsing
issue_body:
Currently we have a big pain point that we cannot re-run a single "build" within a job on Appveyor.  I found the same true on Travis with parallel stages.  We can do it for Travis through the UI, but not appveyor. 

Conan package tools commit message parsing is a useful feature with a very small scope. 

Now that we are parsing commit messages with CPT, and applying filtering of which builds to run as a result, it seems feasible to extend it to be able to specify something like the following:

`[build_policy=<query>]`

where `<query>` uses the syntax of the `conan search` command for example:

`[build_policy=os=Windows AND compiler.version=15 AND build_type=Release]`

With this, we would have precision control over triggering rebuilds any CI system, regardless of it's internal featureset. 

The code and unit testing to be added for query parsing and the filtering layer is non-trivial, so a lot of people would have to vote for this feature to justify it.  While non-trivial, it is certainly feasible, and we could potentially borrow some code from Conan client.

issue_title: [MeshForwarder] Incorrect link-layer address selection
issue_body:
I found that there are couple violations to Thread Specification when it comes to link-layer address selection (in `MeshForwarder::UpdateIp6Route`).

Hightlights:
 - REED sends both link-local and real-local scope multicast message using IEEE 802.15.4 broadcast, however it must transmit message as IEEE 802.15.4 unicast to its parent.
 - When End Device sends realm-local scope multicast (e.g. from RLOC as a source) it uses extended mac addresses on IEEE 802.15.4 while short would be more efficient.
 - `UpdateIp6Route` performs link-layer address selection based on FTD or MTD type of the node, while in some situations this should be done based on current role (Router or End Device - root cause for 1).

I am going to propose some fixes for this issues.

issue_title: sio_magic native 3 conversion script
issue_body:
Some odd things are happening in this conversion.  I've been working with the data_files/Measurement_Import/sio_magic/sio_af_example.dat

Thing one:

It looks like specimen, sample, site, and location tables are all getting populated with a bunch of empty records.  The programs seems to be adding a record to SpecRecs for each MagRec, most of which are empty.  So, right before the specimen table is created, SpecRecs looks like this:

    SpecRecs [{'sample': 'sc12b1', 'specimen': 'sc12b1'}, {}, {}, {}, {}, {}, {}, {}, {}, {}, {}, {}, {}, {}, {}, {}, {}, {}, {}, {}, {}, {}]

This then results in the MagIC tables being all wonky.  


Thing two:

Also, I made a few changes in how the contribution is created, so these lines aren't necessary:

    con.add_empty_magic_table('specimens')
    con.add_empty_magic_table('samples')
    con.add_empty_magic_table('sites')
    con.add_empty_magic_table('locations')
    con.add_empty_magic_table('measurements')

What that does is to add a table to the contribution of the proper type with default required headers and/or headers you specify (so that you can build up a table piece by piece with MagicDataFrame.add), but you are overwriting most of those defaults anyway by assigning df in these lines: 

    con.tables['specimens'].df = DataFrame(SpecRecs)
    # etc.

later.  

This ends up missing some stuff that should happen in the MagicDataFrame \_\_init\_\_, like making sure the index is set correctly.  To address this I added in a data parameter to the MagicDataFrame \_\_init\_\_.  So I changed sio_magic to add MagicDataFrames in a one-liner:

    con.tables['specimens'] = MagicDataFrame(dtype='specimens', data=SpecRecs)

This part two isn't an action item for you, I just wanted to explain what I've done.  I may do similar edits in some of the other conversion scripts as well.  Sorry that functionality was missing before, but I think that this should work better.  

I would appreciate your input on item one, though.

issue_title: Function empty_aligned fails on certain dtypes...
issue_body:
When building the FFT, the function `builders._utils._Xfftn` is eventually called. This calls `builders._utils._compute_array_shapes` to determine the appropriate `input_shape` and `output_shape` and then eventually `pyfftw.empty_aligned(output_shape, output_dtype)`. We were getting the following exception when building large FFTs on very large `complex64` arrays:

    ValueError: negative dimensions are not allowed

This was eventually traced to the `builders._compute_array_shapes` call which produced a tuple of `int32` dimensions for the respected shape tuples. I assume they were `int32` as a consequence of working on `complex64` arrays. In any event, the negative dimensions were probably due to integer wrapping for numbers greater than the capacity of `int32`. See the following instructive example, which was carried out using the pre-built `pyFFTW-0.10.1-cp34-none-win_amd64.whl`::

    >>> dtype = np.dtype(np.complex64)
    >>> shape = (np.int32(2**14), np.int32(2**14))
    >>> pyfftw.empty_aligned(shape, dtype)
    
    ValueError: negative dimensions are not allowed

And a little bit of tweaking...

    >>> shape = (np.int32(2**14 - 1), np.int32(2**14))
    >>> pyfftw.empty_aligned(shape, dtype)
    array([[ 0.+0.j,  0.+0.j,  0.+0.j, ...,  0.+0.j,  0.+0.j,  0.+0.j],
           [ 0.+0.j,  0.+0.j,  0.+0.j, ...,  0.+0.j,  0.+0.j,  0.+0.j],
           [ 0.+0.j,  0.+0.j,  0.+0.j, ...,  0.+0.j,  0.+0.j,  0.+0.j],
           ..., 
           [ 0.+0.j,  0.+0.j,  0.+0.j, ...,  0.+0.j,  0.+0.j,  0.+0.j],
           [ 0.+0.j,  0.+0.j,  0.+0.j, ...,  0.+0.j,  0.+0.j,  0.+0.j],
           [ 0.+0.j,  0.+0.j,  0.+0.j, ...,  0.+0.j,  0.+0.j,  0.+0.j]], dtype=complex64)

In order to perform the required FFTs, I changed `builders._compute_array_shapes` so that it **always** casts the shape to a tuple of Python `int`s rather than something based on the provided `dtype`. For us anyway, there does not seem to be a good reason for the the shape tuple to be anything other than high capacity integers... the memory footprint is obviously dominated by the `dtype` of the actual array rather than that of its metadata.

I just wanted to bring this to your attention... I didn't know if this was expected behavior or not. In any event, we did have to make a hack in your package, which we always try to avoid! :)

issue_title: CSV Column Rename
issue_body:
Hello @onyxfish ,

thanks for csvkit and your other cool tools like agate and proof. 

I wanted to ask if you would integrate a new part into csvkit. 

### CSVRename

`csvrename` would allow you to change the header columns of your dataset. agate has a similar tool for `agate.Table` and I often have to rename columns.

Right now I'm using the `header` shell script from the book [Data Science for the Command Line Toolkit](https://github.com/jeroenjanssens/data-science-at-the-command-line/blob/master/tools/header)
It works but everybody has to install it by themselfes so I cannot share my data pipelines(Makefiles). 

I already created a `csvrename` and it would work as follow:

#### Rename/Replace all headers
Replace all the colum headers with new ones, as long as the list has the same length as the columns.

```
csvrename -n e,d,c,b,a
```

#### Rename specific column headers

```
csvrename -n d,c -c b,a
```

I potentially would add another argument to select the columns by index:
``` 
csvrename -n d,c -i 2,1
```

What do you think? Or do you have another easy way to do it?
Thanks.

issue_title: quit command doesn't really quit when issued from within a python script
issue_body:
In general, all cmd2 commands are scriptable within a Python script via "cmd('command args')".  However, since when running within a Python script we are actually within the do_py command, quit doesn't actually quit the cmd2 application.

The current behavior is non-intuitive.  Issuing a cmd('quit') should really quit.

Making this work will likely require a little bit of plumbing so that do_py returns a boolean value.

issue_body: Right now if you ask for a DataFrame output with `df_out=True`, you lose the information in the DataFrame's index. Since Sklearn transformers may not add or remove rows, it makes sense to retain the input DataFrame's index when `df_out=True`.

issue_title: PyPy 5.9.0 segfault under multiple inheritance
issue_body:
The test suite segfaults under PyPy 5.9.0, in particular when using multiple inheritance of both a Python and C++ class with the Python class first.  It seems in such a case our custom `tp_new` (in `pybind11_object`) doesn't get called, so the instance doesn't get allocated or initialized.

I'm creating this issue to document/track it; more details are at the upstream bug report: https://bitbucket.org/pypy/pypy/issues/2674/cpyext-tp_new-not-called-in-some-multiple

issue_title: Not finding vhosts on Amazon Linux
issue_body:
## My operating system is (include version):

Amazon Linux 2017.03

## I installed Certbot with (certbot-auto, OS package manager, pip, etc):

certbot-auto

## I ran this command and it produced this output:

```
sudo env PATH=$PATH ./certbot-auto --apache --debug -v -d example.org
...
Could not reverse map the HTTPS VirtualHost to the original
...
```

## Certbot's behavior differed from what I expected because:

Certbot should be able to find the vhost.

This issue was created in response to https://community.letsencrypt.org/t/rate-limited-after-trying-multiple-solutions-to-could-not-reverse-map-the-https-virtualhost-to-the-original-issue/37252. Adding `--apache-vhost-root` causes the "Could not reverse map..." error message to go away and be replaced by:

> No vhost exists with servername or alias of: example.org (or it's in a file with multiple vhosts, which Certbot can't parse yet). No vhost was selected. Please specify ServerName or ServerAlias in the Apache config, or split vhosts into separate files.

I played with this myself for a bit in Docker using http24 installed in the default paths and even with `--apache-vhost-root`, we're not finding the vhost. The return value from `certbot_apache.configurator.get_virtual_hosts()` is an empty list.

@joohoi, since Amazon Linux is Red Hat based, this might be a good test to see how better support for non-Debian systems is coming in Apache.

issue_title: Certbot fails to bind on port 443 when there is no deafult Listen
issue_body:
I was trying to install a Let's encrypt certificate on a site which is hosted on Apache in a RedHat Enterprise Linux 6.8.
The server hosts several sites, each one with its IP, and Apache doesn't listen on port 80 or 443 by default.
Each site has its file in conf.d directory, which defines the Virtual Host and Listen on the right port:

*site.conf*

    Listen w.x.y.z.:80
    NameVirtualHost w.x.y.z:80
    <VirtualHost w.x.y.z.:80>
    ...
    </VirtualHost>

In this situation, the first run of certbot didn't modified the configuration to listen on port 443, so the verification failed.
Moreover, since certbot stores the challenge configuration file in conf.d, with RedHat standard file structure this is included twice, one in `Include conf.d/*.conf` in the middle of httpd.conf and the second in `Include /etc/httpd/conf.d/le_tls_sni_01_cert_challenge.conf` at the end as a result of certbot's modifications.

I recently got this error trying to issue a cert:

`The client sent an unacceptable anti-replay nonce :: JWS has invalid anti-replay nonce`

This happens from time to time - the server loses track of outstanding nonces. Since the reply always includes a fresh nonce, the client should auto-retry the request.

issue_title: Test the mediator state machine
JSONRPCClient's nonce got out of sync and spammed logs with nonce updates without actually updating them
issue_body:
## Problem Definition

On a testing session, after trying to do a deposit, which required an on-chain transaction, my Raiden node logs started spamming something like the following line in the stdout:
```
updated nonce from server          [raiden.network.rpc.client]  local=161 server=160
updated nonce from server          [raiden.network.rpc.client]  local=161 server=160
updated nonce from server          [raiden.network.rpc.client]  local=161 server=160
updated nonce from server          [raiden.network.rpc.client]  local=161 server=160
```
Maybe around 10-30x/sec.
The transaction didn't go through, nor the node code be interacted with anymore.
A kernel update required a reboot right after, and I couldn't reproduce the issue anymore (did 3 deposits and a channel-close after it).

Looking at the code, it became clear that the current nonce-update logic was not optimal, and a `cachetools.(TTLCache|cachedmethod)` could put it in a more consistent and reliable state. This led to the PR #1936 , which should fix this issue and possibly allow proper parallel-submitting of transactions.

The test probably occured with master on a2f5fda23b1ab61ecc20b5cca0169a19359c0f0a

issue_title: Thinc GPU capability on Windows - fails to compile
issue_body:
It looks like Thinc has some hard time making GPU computation available if used on Windows.

Similar issues were raised in spaCy and thinc:
https://github.com/explosion/spaCy/issues/1745
https://github.com/explosion/thinc/issues/50

It seems related in how the build is set-up in setup.py:
https://github.com/explosion/thinc/blob/master/setup.py#L56

Based on quick search, others are struggling with this as well:
https://github.com/rbgirshick/py-faster-rcnn/issues/11

Some more info (they came to a working solution there):
https://github.com/cudamat/cudamat/issues/46#issuecomment-73803650

Will research a bit more later.

issue_title: Use of 'nonlocal' keyword breaks Python2 compatibility
issue_body:
Usage of thinc (and therefore, spaCy) is currently not possible on Python2 because the `nonlocal` keyword is used twice:

```bash
$ git grep nonlocal
thinc/neural/_classes/rnn.py:            nonlocal Zs, d_pad, dWx, dWh, db
thinc/neural/_classes/rnn.py:            nonlocal dW, db
```

If I got it right, these changes were introduced with commits 58f7c157cb592539bca8b71e640b1a30a2eda57a and 456177aba82abac6ab7df2c62454e5ef8a55c035 from  2017-06-18 and 2017-06-21, respectively.

I can provide a patch if a preferred workaround is suggested.

issue_title: Behavior of remote targets on local host.
issue_body:
Just to clarify ( #475 ), `remote` targets currently work like this:

1. A `remote` target always `exist()` (returns `true`) locally.
2. A `remote` target, like other non-file targets, are not translated during task preparation.
3. `remote(target)` are replaced with `target` at the beginning of `execute_task`.

It is then clear that `remote` targets only works properly inside tasks. It is not possible to do something like 

```
input: remote('*.txt'), group_by=1
```
although

```
input: remote('file.txt'), remote('file2.txt'), group_by=1
```
works.

You also cannot do

```
input: remote('a.txt'):
sh:
    touch ${input}  
```
since `remote('a.txt')` is not a proper filename, although

```
input: remote('a.txt'):
task:
sh:
    touch ${input}  
```
works all right.

I am not sure if there is any case we should handle `remote` target locally.

issue_title: Improved error info for step processes
issue_body:
This is a complementary issue to #834 : I recently got user feedback complaining that debugging in step process is difficult, when writing python scripts directly into SoS steps. For example:

```
[global]
def bad_print():
    print(f'{[1,2,3](9)}')

[1]
print("hi from SoS")
bad_print()
```

The error message is:

```
ERROR: [default_1]: TypeError at line 2 of 
    print("hi from SoS")
>>> bad_print()
'list' object is not callable
```

The problem is that the user has no idea which line of `bad_print()` complains. `-v4` gives:

```
    exec(script, _dict)
  File "<string>", line 2, in <module>
  File "<string>", line 2, in bad_print
TypeError: 'list' object is not callable
```

In this MWE it is trivial to realize that indeed the 2nd line of `[global]` is problematic. But when `global` gets complicated and without any line numbers in SoS Notebook it is difficult to determine where the problem is. The `<string>` can be different from the physical text in SoS script. This MWE might not be very convincing but I hope I've conveyed the idea. I'll try come up with better examples if something more convincing is needed. The error that the user actually has run into was impossible to pin-point that he ended up copying the entire thing to a pure python notebook to debug.

issue_title: Services from `/arlas/explore/*` accepting a filter in the `GET` or `POST` should also accept a filter in the `HEADER`
issue_body:
Services from `/arlas/explore/*` accepting a filter in the `GET` or `POST` should also accept a filter in the `HEADER`.
The filter is specified in the JSON format and a `AND` operator is applied between the filter provided in the GET/POST and the filter provided in the `HEADER`.

Note: In some cases, the filter is currently appended as `OR`, if for example two `equal` operators are applied on a same field. This behavior is not acceptable for the present case : a real  `AND` operator must be applied.

issue_title: Display a log rather than raise a FnapyPricingError when a list of eans is provided
issue_body: In the previous versions of `fnapy`, only one EAN could be passed to `query_pricing` and it made sense to raise an exception when something was wrong. Now, `query_pricing` accepts a list of EANs and raising the exception would prevent the user from having the results even if only one EAN is not found.

issue_title: Add tips for development to documentation.
issue_body:
We should add some tips for development to the documentation. Some things that came up recently.

1. If you are experiencing a test failure, you can run an individual test as follows.

    ```
    python test/runtest.py APITest.testKeywordArgs
    ```

2. Note that usually only the first test failure matters. Once one test fails, often all subsequent tests fail. We should fix this.

3. You should install Ray with `python setup.py develop` (in `ray/python/`). That way, when you change the Python code, you won't need to reinstall Ray. However, if you change the C++ code, you will still need to recompile (this can be done with `./build.sh` (in `ray/`).

4. If you want to directly inspect the values in the *primary Redis shard*, you can query it with commands like

    ```python
    r = ray.worker.global_worker.redis_client
    r.keys("*")
    ```

issue_title: Scripts for building wheels do not work.
[rllib] Sample throughput count in async_samples_optimizer.py
issue_body:
Hi,

analogue to the fix of measuring samples throughput in https://github.com/ray-project/ray/commit/f31a6ca96510deb77117826437434da80e6df1f4

shouldn't https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/master/python/ray/rllib/optimizers/async_samples_optimizer.py#L231

```

        with self.timers["sample_processing"]:
            for ev, sample_batch in self.sample_tasks.completed():
                self._check_not_multiagent(sample_batch)
                sample_timesteps += self.sample_batch_size
```

also use the actual sample batch size as to not overestimate throughput, depending on the packing strategy in the sampler?

issue_title: What is the greatest limitation of Ray?
issue_body:
I recently analyzed the architecture of ray. I found ray is a greate, simple and user friendly product. I even can not find a disadvantage of ray. But, I think all products have the trade-off.  What is limitation of Ray when using with deep learning or machine learning?
-

issue_title: Youtube Live stream is playing up to 5 minutes before session.streams() stops returning None
issue_body:
### Checklist

- [x] This is a bug report.
- [ ] This is a feature request.
- [ ] This is a plugin (improvement) request.
- [x ] I have read the contribution guidelines.

### Description
Platform: Raspberry Pi 3 - Stretch
Encoder: Adobe Flash Media Live Encoder 3.2
Encoder Setup URL I: As per Youtube recommendations for 720p video H264 streams.
Encoder Setup URL II: 30 fps, 320x240 USB2.0 Webcam, 500Kbps, H264 streams.
Streamlink: 0.10.0
Python: 2.7
URL I: https://youtube.com/user/ApostolicFaithHQ/live
Live Sundays 11AM and 6PM, Tues and Fri 7:30PM
URL II: https://www.youtube.com/channel/UC6Q5UfqUUsmsW59m8xZSc8Q/live
Injection site for test. Time is intermittent throughout the day.

Code flow overview:
Python script uses the Streamlink API to query either of the URLs above every 15 seconds until session.streams() returns other than None.  The script then searches for a certain key in the streams object  to determine if the stream is Live or is something Youtube seems to put at the URL when a recent Live video has ended and at the time it is stored to the video archive.  If the key is not found the process repeats indefinitely.
When the key is found, the script launches a subprocess.Popen to start a CLI version of Streamlink with omxplayer (because I am a bit of a NOOB and haven't figured out how to stream to omxplayer with the API).  When the Live stream has ended, the python script does some cleanup and repeats the infinite query.

Use Case A: Happy
The video stream has been started for sometime _before_ streamlink/python script is started.  The query of the URL at 15 second intervals, 'finds' a live stream within 30 seconds (sooner if the interval timer is reduced). The video plays perfectly for the entire session without freeze, jitter, etc.

Use Case B: Sad
The video stream is started any time _after_ the streamlink/python script begins its query of the URL at 15 second intervals.  In this case, session.streams() returns None for upto 5.5 minutes after the video stream has started.  Once the video finally starts, it plays perfectly for the entire session without freeze, jitter, etc.

In either Use Case, a browser (on Windows 7) will begin showing the live stream immediately.

### Expected / Actual behavior
The expectation is that Use Case B stops returning None within a few seconds of a Live stream starting.

### Reproduction steps / Explicit stream URLs to test
1. Start the streamlink/python script _before_ the Live stream begins, using URL I or URL II and 'best' as parameters. ie: AFC_Streamer.py URL I  best
2. Start the Live stream using the Encoder noted above..
3. Observe that a typical interval before session.streams() returns data other than None is 2.5 to 5.5 minutes.

### Logs
[afc_streamer_Sad.log](https://github.com/streamlink/streamlink/files/1746333/afc_streamer_Sad.log)
[afc_streamer_Happy.log](https://github.com/streamlink/streamlink/files/1746334/afc_streamer_Happy.log)
Python Script

issue_title: Update OAuth tokens
issue_body:
### Checklist

- [x] This is a bug report.
- [ ] This is a feature request.
- [ ] This is a plugin (improvement) request.
- [x] I have read the contribution guidelines.

### Description

Earlier today Travis.ci reported an issue related to key vulnerabilities (https://blog.travis-ci.com/2017-05-08-security-advisory). We should make sure any keys that could have potentially been leaked are updated. 

I believe this impacts the following aspects:

- Travis CI deployment key used by GitHub for Streamlink tests

And potentially impacts the following:

However, clickhouse-driver instead throws an exception:

```python
record = {'x': 1}
client.execute('DROP TABLE IF EXISTS test')

issue_title: Uses of self.nodes[-1] cause false positives
issue_body:
A little bummed out I didn't realize this until I investigated the failure of the `test_orelse` test on [my branch](https://github.com/python-security/pyt/compare/testing_djangonv#diff-c7870eaa2a6e2a6edee14e0deeccfac6R209), oh well.

Here are the relevant test case and output.

```python
def does_this_kill_us(diff):
	return subprocess.call(diff, shell=True)

@app.route('/poc', methods=['POST'])
def poc():
	try:
	    value = None
	except ImportError:
	    value = request.args.get('foo')
	else:
	    does_this_kill_us(value)
```

output with -trim flag on.
```python
Vulnerability 1:
File: example/example_inputs/try_orelse.py
 > User input at line 9, trigger word "get(": 
	value = request.args.get('foo')
Reassigned in: 
	File: example/example_inputs/try_orelse.py
	 > Line 11: temp_1_diff = value
	File: example/example_inputs/try_orelse.py
	 > Line 1: diff = temp_1_diff
File: example/example_inputs/try_orelse.py
 > reaches line 2, trigger word "subprocess.call(": 
	ret_does_this_kill_us = subprocess.call(diff,shell=True)
```

Where does it connect the basic block of the source to does_this_kill_us? 
That would be in `save_local_scope` where self.nodes[-1] here is the last node that was added, which is `Label: value = request.args.get('foo')`
```python
            previous_node = self.nodes[-1]
            r = RestoreNode(save_name + ' = ' + assignment.left_hand_side,
                            save_name, [assignment.left_hand_side],
                            line_number=line_number, path=self.filenames[-1])
            saved_scope_node = self.append_node(r)

            saved_variables.append(SavedVariable(LHS=save_name,
                                                 RHS=assignment.left_hand_side))
            previous_node.connect(saved_scope_node)
```


Okay, how should we fix it?
Well we currently only use the [-1] node when we're adding a function (in master this means a user-defined function, in my branch it means a user defined function or a blackbox/builtin function) so I guess in say, `handle_or_else` in base_cfg.py we either (a) pass in what should be the node we want to connect to e.g. `value = None` or (b) connect the 2 nodes in e.g. `handle_or_else`. These are both a lot of work though because we have to do something special in everywhere a function can be called.

issue_title: Wrong endpoint used in metadata when specifying viewset
issue_body:
Hi,
When an endpoint is registered with a specific viewset, a different endpoint is generated in determine_metadata (hence losing all out custom attributes). The responsible part in metada.py:
```
        try:
            serializer_instance = view.get_serializer()
        except Exception:
            # Custom viewset is expecting something we can't guess
            serializer_instance = view.get_serializer_class()()
        endpoint = None
        if hasattr(view, 'endpoint'):
            endpoint = view.endpoint
        else:
            if hasattr(view.get_serializer_class().Meta, 'model'):
                from .endpoints import Endpoint
                endpoint = Endpoint(view.get_serializer_class().Meta.model, viewset=view)
```
The endpoint relation is normally set by the viewset_factory, and it's not when specifying a viewset. 
Came up with this, not ideal, fix :
```
class Endpoint(with_metaclass(EndpointMetaClass, BaseEndpoint)):

    def __init__(self, model=None, **kwargs):
        self.inflector = Inflector(self.inflector_language)

        if model is not None:
            self.model = model

        arg_names = ('fields', 'serializer', 'permission_classes', 'filter_fields', 'search_fields',
                     'viewset', 'read_only', 'include_str', 'ordering_fields', 'page_size',
                     'base_viewset', 'fields_annotation', 'fieldsets', 'base_serializer', 'list_me')
        for arg_name in arg_names:
            setattr(self, arg_name, kwargs.pop(arg_name, getattr(self, arg_name, None)))
            if arg_name == 'viewset' and getattr(self, arg_name):
                assert getattr(self.viewset, 'endpoint', None) is None, 'This viewset is already registered by an endpoint'
                setattr(self.viewset, 'endpoint', self)
```
I think we have to prevent the same viewset to be registered by different endpoints or the metadata woulnd't mean anything. We could also test the class instead of the objects.. (The tests will not pass as the same viewset is registered multiple times for now)

issue_title: Adding Virtual Products module to datacube
issue_body:
## Background

Many common use cases of Datacube (DC) involve combining temporally and spatially similar sets of data to produce output data. An example is using Product Quality (PQ) products with other products to perform cloud masking. Issues have been encountered with dataset merging previously in Datacube stats (see https://gist.github.com/Kirill888/a4b52d0077fa4e36b351c22827782492) and in Datacube WMS there are times where only TIRS sensor datasets are available, causing unexpected results. As such the creation of Virtual Products (VPs) has been considered as a way to provide users a way to easily define common patterns used in Datacube work and to solve issues seen in software which uses Datacube surrounding the merging and transformation of data without having to create bespoke code each time.


## Products in Datacube
In the current version of DC, products are unique strings which associate that string (the product name) with metadata about Datasets. This metadata includes information such as the measurements that the product contains, the grid specification and the mapping of raw fields in the dataset to computed fields (e.g. from_dt and to_dt which product a time measurement). In DC the products are represented by a `DatasetType` which has a `MetadataType`. Each `Dataset` has a `DatasetType` and the `DatasetType` and `MetadataType` are typically loaded from JSON documents stored in a database. See also (http://damien-agdc.readthedocs.io/en/extradocs/dev/data_model.html)

## Virtual Products

### Class Thoughts

A class implementing Virtual Products (VPs) will represent a tree which represents the hierarchy of virtual products. Leaf nodes will be Virtual Products which are unmodified products.

Combinators
    Combinators are functions which accept 1 or more Virtual Products and return a Virtual Product. The following combinators will be available:

    `Collate`: `List A -> A`
        For example: Combining the sensor readings for LS5 on 05/07/95, LS6 on 06/11/95, LS8 on 07/11/95 into one dataset.

    `Juxtapose`: `A -> B -> A x B`
        Similar to a `JOIN` in SQL, could be outer or inner. This could be used for apply PQ for cloud masking in combination with Filter or Transform.

    `Drop`: `A -> A`
        Given some predicate function, this will remove all datasets with metadata that do not meet the given predicate. Performed at query time.

    `Filter`: `A -> A`
        Given some predicate function, this will remove all points in a dataset that do not meet the given predicate. Performed at fetch time.

    `Transform`: `A -> B`
        Synonymous with the `map` function of the `mapreduce` paradigm. Will require some transformation function f which accepts a dataset and returns a dataset. This combinator may also modify the type of the dataset.

Methods & Workflow (High Level API)

    `construct`
        Constructing a Virtual Product will set it's child Virtual Products as well as any functions it requires to operate. The construction will not access the database.

    `validate`
        The validation function will check the measurement types of the inputs and outputs and ensure that they are compatible. In most cases this will be checking that they match, however with a `Transform` there may be a change.

    `query`
        The query function will retrieve datasets which match a query, product, and in case of `Drop` products, metadata predicate. The product being queried may be virtual. Access to the database will cease after this stage.

    `serialize`
        Optional(?) The serialize function will use the results of the `query` function (i.e. datasets that match the query) and serialize them for use on machines or nodes that cannot or should not query the database but can fetch data.

    `fetch`
        The fetch function will use the results of the `query`, direct or deserialized, and load the datasets. At this stage combinators working on data can apply their functions or predicates; for example `Filter` can execute. Once completed, the output for the top level Virtual Product will be the desired Virtual Dataset.

## Potential Approaches

### Virtual Product Module

This approach would not modify any opendatacube-core code. Instead a new module would be created that defined `VirtualProduct`. This module would make use of the `datacube` module and API.

The module would query the properties of the `DatasetTypes` (a.k.a. products) used in the definition of the `VirtualProduct` and conduct type and sanity checking on combinators. After passing the checks the storage location of datasets for the given query (e.g. geoboxed or time bounded), the database would be queried using `datacube`. Advanced versions could intelligently walk the product tree to determine how to construct a SQL query for the various products and / or cache query results.

The module would then load the datasets from their location (e.g. disk, aws s3, etc.). Once the datasets are loaded, combinators are applied to the datasets and the results until the final result is created and returned. Again intelligent caching of result could be applied in this stage.

### Potential ideas / constraints for features (in progress)

- Metadata as bands, e.g. data about what LandSat data came from may be encoded as metadata which will be preserved as a band
- Timestamps must be retained
- Must support DC GridWorkflow for datasets which will not fit into memory
- For all queries against a virtual product the database should be accessed to retrieve the locations of datasets, and then database should not be accessed again. The locations of the datasets may need to be serialized to a storage device and loaded later.
- Support querying multiple databases.

issue_title: Is there an easier way to cancel groups of tasks?
issue_body:
I am struggling with a good way to manage groups of tasks.  The best I've come up with seems a bit convoluted, involving a factory function.  Something along the lines of:
```
def cancellable_factory(func, cancel_scopes, shutdown_event=None, desc=""):
    async def scope_func():
        try:
            print(desc,"started")
            with trio.open_cancel_scope() as cancel_scope:
                cancel_scopes.append(cancel_scope)
                await func()
        except Exception as exc:
            print(desc, "crashed: {!r}".format(exc))
            if shutdown_event:
                shutdown_event.set()
        print(desc, "stopped")
    return scope_func

async def wait_shutdown(cancel_scopes, shutdown_event):
    await shutdown_event.wait()
    for cancel_scope in cancel_scopes:
        cancel_scope.cancel()

cancel_scopes = []
shutdown_event = trio.Event()
nursery.spawn(cancellable_factory(blah_a, cancel_scopes, shutdown_event, "blahA"))
nursery.spawn(cancellable_factory(blah_b, cancel_scopes, shutdown_event, "blahB"))
nursery.spawn(cancellable_factory(blah_c, cancel_scopes, shutdown_event, "blahC"))
nursery.spawn(wait_shutdown, cancel_scopes, shutdown_event)
```
Am I missing an easy way to manage groups of tasks (eg associated with each connection to a server)?

Is it possible to cancel a task directly instead of tracking separate cancel_scopes?  Something like:
```
def cancellable_factory(func, shutdown_event=None, desc=""):
    async def scope_func():
...
async def wait_shutdown(tasks, shutdown_event):
    await shutdown_event.wait()
    for task in tasks:
        task.cancel()

taska = nursery.spawn(cancellable_factory(blah_a, shutdown_event, "blahA"))
taskb = nursery.spawn(cancellable_factory(blah_b, shutdown_event, "blahB"))
taskc = nursery.spawn(cancellable_factory(blah_c, shutdown_event, "blahC"))
nursery.spawn(wait_shutdown, (taska, taskb, taskc), shutdown_event)
```

issue_title: Watch CRD receives the same event
issue_body:
It seems that when watching CRD custom objects we keep receiving the same event.

here is how to reproduce, `kubectl create -f` the following CRD.

```
kind: CustomResourceDefinition
metadata:
  name: foobars.yoyo.com
spec:
  group: yoyo.com
  names:
    kind: foobar
    listKind: foobarList
    plural: foobars
    singular: foobar
  scope: Namespaced
  version: v1beta1
```

Then run this watch script:

```
from kubernetes import client, config, watch

GROUP = "yoyo.com"
VERSION = "v1beta1"
PLURAL = "foobars"

config.load_kube_config()

crds = client.CustomObjectsApi()
        
resource_version = ""

while True:

    print "initializing stream"
    stream = watch.Watch().stream(crds.list_cluster_custom_object,
                                  GROUP, VERSION, PLURAL,
                                  resource_version=resource_version)
    for event in stream:
        t = event["type"]
        obj = event["object"]
    
        print t
        print obj  

        # Configure where to resume streaming.
        metadata = obj['metadata']

        if metadata['resourceVersion'] is not None:
            resource_version = metadata['resourceVersion']
            print resource_version
```

and create a custom object like so:

```
apiVersion: yoyo.com/v1beta1
kind: foobar
metadata:
  name: test
spec:
  whatever: youwant
```

The script stdout will be:

```
$ python ./bibi.py 
initializing stream
ADDED
{u'kind': u'foobar', u'spec': {}, u'apiVersion': u'yoyo.com/v1beta1', u'metadata': {u'name': u'test', u'clusterName': u'', u'namespace': u'default', u'resourceVersion': u'84148', u'creationTimestamp': u'2018-04-04T14:07:40Z', u'selfLink': u'/apis/yoyo.com/v1beta1/namespaces/default/foobars/test', u'uid': u'89a26340-3811-11e8-816b-0800272f923d'}}
84148
ADDED
{u'kind': u'foobar', u'spec': {}, u'apiVersion': u'yoyo.com/v1beta1', u'metadata': {u'name': u'test', u'clusterName': u'', u'namespace': u'default', u'resourceVersion': u'84148', u'creationTimestamp': u'2018-04-04T14:07:40Z', u'selfLink': u'/apis/yoyo.com/v1beta1/namespaces/default/foobars/test', u'uid': u'89a26340-3811-11e8-816b-0800272f923d'}}
84148
ADDED
```

The stream does not die so the resource version never gets reset. The for loop keeps getting the same event over and over.

I was expecting the event to be received only once.

issue_title: Specification for float16 serialization should be more specific
issue_body:
We have an existing specification for how float16 values should be serialized in the `TensorProto` within the `int32_data` field: https://github.com/onnx/onnx/blob/master/onnx/onnx.proto#L325. It says:

```
  // float16 values must be bit-wise converted to an uint16_t prior
  // to writing to the buffer.
```

One can infer that each value must be zero-filled in the top 32 bits, but it's not explicitly stated. In fact, I haven't found any implementations for this behavior. We should more explicitly specify that this is the case.

In addition, we should clarify the behavior for `float16` values when serialized to the `raw_data` field. Are they stored in the 32-bit wide format?

issue_title: AlmostEqual or Extend Equal Operator
issue_body:
Looks like most framework support Equal on floating tensors...
ONNX may support this with different op (AlmostEqual) or by adding an optional attribute.

I personally prefer the latter.

issue_title: Multiple Operator Sets in ModelProto for One Domain
issue_body: Sometimes, we create multiple operator set for one single domain, for example, (domain_name, version)=('', 1), ('', 2), and ('', 6). It's better to have only one with the highest version number, ('', 6), to avoid confusion.

issue_body: The documentation states that pip install velocyto should be enough. This is incorrect. As per conda, all the deps will need to be installed first.

issue_title: Would be nice if @profile and friends also showed peak usage per line
issue_body:
The line by line view shows memory when the line finishes executing, and increment. However, if the line is calling a function, that function may internally allocate far more memory and then deallocate it. So the report might say "Max 500MB, increment 450MB" for a line, but actual peak was 1500MB... and there's no way to tell the peak came from that line.

If peak was also reported with each line, you'd be able to say "oh, that function is doing a bunch of allocation internally, I should profile it too."

issue_title: Use sub-interface adapters if possible
`confirm_redirect_uri` does not get the default redirect URI
issue_body:
When a client doesn't request a `redirect_uri` in an authorization request, `oauthlib` calls `get_default_redirect_uri`, and passes this to `save_authorization_code`. So far, so good.

However, when the redirect is performed, `confirm_redirect_uri` is called with `None` as `redirect_uri`. This doesn't match the `redirect_uri` saved earlier. Is this intended behavior? It felt wrong for me to have to check for `None` myself, and repeat getting the default URI from the client datastruct again.

issue_title: Questions for efsearch param, Thrift server
issue_body:
Hi,

I have a question about how/if a client of a Thrift query server using HNSW can set the efsearch query parameter per KNN query. Are clients expected to set it at index using [SetQueryTimeParams](https://github.com/nmslib/nmslib/blob/d3cc0899604698f1e78867cf5396031268db55f1/similarity_search/include/method/multi_index.h#L58) before sending each query? I am worried that will cause various concurrency issues, from read/write error to race condition.

Thank you!

issue_body:
Hi

I'm in the need of having nested data in my current tests. So basically I was looking for a way to nest "for" iteration over provided @data. Is this possible? how? 
I could create a for in my test itself, but I would lose proper test map in statistics. I could create a better log system to being able to distinguish among which specific test failed, but if it's possible to nest @data, that would help me a lot.

Thanks a lot for you help and attention.

Regards

issue_title: Random crashes with single-threaded usage of jpype (SIGSEGV)
issue_body:
In a variety of environments i386, amd64, Debian Stretch, Debian Buster, Ubuntu 16.04, Ubuntu 18.04, jpype package provided by Debian, jpype provided from this repository (master) I'm having a random crash issue (needs about 2 to 20 tries depending on environment) when using a custom Java Library which in the background invokes the Oracle 8 JDBC driver (which uses multiple threads).
python reports thread.active_count() = 1 at all times, cat /proc/<pid>/status reports 1 thread before start of JVM, 12 threads after start of JVM, 15 threads after successful data-retrieval through the custom library and oracle jdbc (the number remains at 12 if the custom library is used to retrieve data through jtds, it seems ojdbc does some threading internally). Sometimes the crash happens at the first retrieval of the data, sometimes it happens later.

It seems somehow that if the python-world is less exhaustive (toy examples of code) then it is more stable (no crash observed). Once I was able to observe a crash when in the background jtds instead of odbc was accessed.

I'd appreciate any advice in creating a reproducible testcase.
[hs_err_pid581.log](https://github.com/jpype-project/jpype/files/2321866/hs_err_pid581.log)

issue_title: Reworking of Query result handling
issue_body:
gromgull, 2011-02-18T21:49:01.000Z 
Purpose of code changes on this branch:

(note there is no branch, only attached patch)

This introduces a richer Result object for queries, it is meant to be backwards compatible to the SPARQLResults we already get from the sparql impl in rdfextras.

It also introduces plugin points for ResultParsers and ResultSerializers 
A matching patch for rdfextras with parser/serializer support for xml and json sparql results is coming shortly.


When reviewing my code changes, please focus on:
Does this break anything? :)

After the review, I'll merge this branch into:
trunk
/trunk

Comment 1 by gromgull
patch


Comment 2 by gromgull
I forgot to mention - this obviously restricts us to the four types of queries defined in the new class, select, ask, describe, construct. I cannot think of any, but perhaps someone else has a use-case where this is problematic?


Comment 3 by gromgull
Clearly it was too late at night for me to remember most of the things I wanted to say about this:

This would also also deprecate the QueryResult plugin stuff we have now. I have problem finding a scenario where you would want to provide a query processor, but NOT a result plugin? Or any where you would want to mix and match? For the sparql implementation in rdfextras, the two are very tightly coupled.

A future patch, (not yet written), will make the rdfextras sparql processor work with this.


Comment 4 by gromgull
new more correct patch :) 

See also http://code.google.com/p/rdfextras/source/list?r=query-result-cleanup


Comment 5 by gromgull
Moved the patch to a hg branch, query-result-cleanup: 

http://code.google.com/p/rdflib/source/browse/?r=query-result-cleanup


Comment 6 by gromgull
Ok - I'll give you until next week, when I am back from holiday I will merge if noone says anything :)


Comment 7 by gromgull
This issue was closed by revision d45003524720.
